md: ## Welcome to PawWalk Academy ã Android edition

By the end of this course you will have built **PawWalk** ã a real Android app where dog owners find walkers, book and pay for walks, and watch their dog's walk live on a map with GPS. It's not a toy: the finished app already lives in this repository at `apps/android`, and every lesson here teaches you to rebuild a real piece of it, file by file.
You need **zero** Kotlin, Android, or Jetpack Compose knowledge to start. We begin with "what is a variable" and end with an AI assistant that can draft a booking for you.

> Already done the iOS track? Great ã the concepts rhyme on purpose (`let` ã `val`, `@State` ã `remember`), so a lot of this will feel like a second pass. Never touched Swift either? Also fine ã this course assumes nothing.
## The app, in one screenshot tour

- **Auth** ã sign up / log in as a dog *owner* or a *walker*. Sessions survive app restarts thanks to encrypted on-device storage.
- **Home** ã a designed dashboard with your walk stats and recent walks.
- **Walkers** ã browse walkers, their ratings and prices, and book one.
- **Bookings** ã create a booking, watch its status move from *pending* ã *confirmed* ã *in progress* ã *completed*.
- **Live tracking** ã a screen showing the walk happening in real time, fed by GPS.
- **AI assistant** ã chat with an assistant that can draft a booking for you.
## How the pieces talk

The Android app is a *client*. It talks HTTP + JSON to a Python backend (FastAPI) that owns the database. The backend is already built and already running in this repo ã your job in this course is the Android side. It's the exact same backend the iOS app talks to.

> You'll see the phrase **API contract** a lot. It's the agreement about exactly what JSON the backend sends and expects ã written down in `docs/API-CONTRACT.md`. The Kotlin code you write will mirror it precisely.

steps.title: Your first look at Kotlin ã MainActivity.kt (trimmed)
steps.caption: This is the real entry point of the app, trimmed to the shape that matters. By the end of this course you'll understand ã and have retyped ã pieces of every line here.

steps.q: Who owns the database in PawWalk?
choices: The Android app stores everything on the phone
The Python backend ã the Android app is a client that talks to it over HTTP
Google Play services
The database lives inside Android Studio
steps.explain: The Android app never touches the database directly ã it sends and receives JSON over HTTP. That separation is why the same backend also serves the iOS app.
steps.nudge: Think about what `docs/API-CONTRACT.md` is for ã why would two different apps need a contract?

md: ## Reading MainActivity.kt, piece by piece
Let's decode that file you just saw, one keyword at a time ã no prior Kotlin needed.

- **`class MainActivity : ComponentActivity()`** ã declares a `class` named `MainActivity` that *inherits from* `ComponentActivity`, Android's base class for a screen with its own lifecycle. The colon means "is a kind of."
- **`override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?)`** ã `fun` declares a function (Kotlin's word for what other languages call a method). `override` means this replaces a function `ComponentActivity` already defines ã Android calls `onCreate` once, when the screen is first created. The `?` after `Bundle` means the parameter is *nullable*: it might be a `Bundle`, or it might be `null`. Much more on that in Module 2.
- **`setContent { ãÎ }`** ã the bridge into Jetpack Compose. Everything inside those curly braces describes the UI declaratively ã you say *what* the screen looks like for the current state, not a sequence of steps to build it.
- **`PawWalkTheme { ãÎ }`, `Surface(...)`** ã these are `@Composable` functions: ordinary Kotlin functions marked as UI-describing. Compose calls them to draw the screen, and re-calls them ("recomposes") whenever the state they read changes.
## Compose vs SwiftUI, side by side
If you've done (or plan to do) the iOS track, the ideas map almost one-to-one:

| Concept | SwiftUI | Jetpack Compose |
|---|---|---|
| UI building block | `struct` conforming to `View` | `@Composable` function |
| Declare a constant | `let` | `val` |
| Declare a variable | `var` | `var` |
| Local UI state | `@State` | `remember { mutableStateOf(...) }` |
| Re-render on state change | automatic re-render | recomposition |
| Vertical stack | `VStack` | `Column` |
| Horizontal stack | `HStack` | `Row` |
| String interpolation | `"\(name)"` | `"$name"` |
Same job, different spelling. You'll see this table's rows again as real code throughout the course.

steps.q: In `override fun onCreate(savedInstanceState: Bundle?)`, what does the `?` after `Bundle` mean?
choices: It's a typo
The parameter is nullable ã it might be a Bundle, or it might be null
It marks the function as optional to call
It means the function returns a Bundle
steps.explain: Kotlin makes nullability part of the type: `Bundle` can never be null, `Bundle?` might be. Android passes `null` here on a fresh launch, and a real `Bundle` when restoring after the OS killed the app.
steps.nudge: Kotlin puts nullability right in the type name ã what job would a `?` do there?

md: ## Learning by typing
Reading about code teaches you to *recognize* it. **Typing** code teaches you to *write* it. So this course makes you type ã a lot. Each lesson mixes four kinds of steps:

- **Explanations** ã short, plain-English. No wall of jargon.
- **Code to read** ã real files from the PawWalk repo, syntax-highlighted.
- **Quick checks** ã one multiple-choice question to make sure an idea landed.
- **Write the code** ã an editor where you type Kotlin and the course checks it.
The **Continue** button under each step unlocks the next one. Quizzes and exercises must be completed before you can continue ã that's the point.

> Your progress (including code you type) is saved in your browser automatically, separately from the iOS course ã the two live side by side. Close the tab, come back tomorrow, and you'll be right where you left off.
## When you get stuck

Fail a check twice and an **"I'm stuck ã show the solution"** link appears. Using it is fine ã but *type the solution out yourself* instead of pasting. The whole value is in your fingers learning the syntax.
Some steps are real-world checklists ã tasks on your own machine, like running the app on an emulator. The browser can't verify those, so you check them off yourself.

steps.q: What's the recommended way to use the "show the solution" feature?
choices: Copy-paste the solution to move on quickly
Read it, then type it out yourself in the editor
Never use it ã struggling for hours builds character
Skip the exercise entirely
steps.explain: Getting unstuck fast is good; typing it yourself is what makes it stick. Struggle a little, then look, then type.

md: ## The tools
Android development needs one big tool and a couple of small ones, all free, on Mac, Windows, or Linux:

- **Android Studio** ã Google's IDE. It bundles the Kotlin compiler, the Android SDK, and the emulator (a virtual Android phone that runs on your computer). It's a big download, so start it first.
- **A Pixel emulator (API 36)** ã Android Studio creates this for you; no physical phone required.
- **Gradle** ã the build tool. You never install it by hand: opening the project in Android Studio downloads the exact version pinned in this repo and syncs automatically.
The backend also needs **uv** (a fast Python package manager) ã one install command and you're done.

lessons.title: Run PawWalk for the first time

md: ## Two processes, one app

To see PawWalk working you run two things: the **backend** (a local web server on port 8000) and the **Android app** (in the emulator). Here's the one Android-specific wrinkle worth knowing before you run anything:

> The emulator is its own little virtual machine ã from its point of view, *your* computer isn't `localhost`, it's a separate machine reachable at the special address **`10.0.2.2`**. So the app is configured with `API_BASE_URL = "http://10.0.2.2:8000"` (you'll find this in `BuildConfig`, generated from `app/build.gradle.kts`) instead of `http://localhost:8000`. Same backend, same port ã just a different address because the emulator sits one network hop away.
> Keep the backend terminal window open while you use the app ã if it's not running, the app falls back to built-in sample data so it never looks broken, but you won't see live changes.

steps.label: Over to Android Studio
steps.title: Run the app in Android Studio
items: Terminal tab 1: `cd apps/backend && uv run fastapi dev` ã leave it running. Open [http://localhost:8000/docs](http://localhost:8000/docs) and admire the auto-generated API docs.
In Android Studio, make sure your Pixel API 36 emulator is selected in the device dropdown in the toolbar.
Click the green ãÑ **Run** button (or press **Shift+F10**) and wait for Gradle to build and the emulator to boot.
Sign up as an owner in the app and look around.
In the app, open the walkers list and confirm walkers load ã that's live data from your backend, delivered over `10.0.2.2`.

steps.q: The app shows sample data instead of real walkers ã what's the most likely cause?
choices: The emulator has no network access at all
The backend isn't running ã `uv run fastapi dev` was stopped or never started
Android Studio needs to be reinstalled
You need to deploy the backend to the internet first
steps.explain: The app is built to never look broken ã if it can't reach `10.0.2.2:8000`, it quietly falls back to sample data instead of crashing. 99% of the time that just means the backend process isn't running.
steps.nudge: Remember: two processes. Which one answers on port 8000?

md: ## You're ready

You've seen the finished product and you have a working toolchain. Next up: the Kotlin language itself ã starting from the very first `val`. Everything you learn will point straight back at code you just watched run.

steps.q: Ready to write some Kotlin?
choices: Let's go ÞÊ
I was born ready
steps.explain: Off we go.
steps.nudge: There is no wrong answer hereãÎ well, almost.

md: ## Naming your data
A program is mostly data with names attached. In Kotlin you attach a name to a value with one of two keywords:

- **`val`** declares a **value** ã a name whose reference is set once and can never be reassigned.
- **`var`** declares a **variable** ã a name whose value you can replace later.

Kotlin strongly prefers `val`. A name that *can't* be reassigned is a name that can't drift out from under you by accident ã a whole category of bugs, gone. Android Studio even warns you when you write `var` for something you never reassign.
> Rule of thumb: start with `val`. Switch to `var` only when the **compiler** ã the program that turns your Kotlin into a runnable app ã tells you the value actually needs to change.

steps.title: val vs var
steps.source:
val dogName = "Mochi"     // a val: set once
val priceCents = 1500     // also a val
var rating = 4.8          // a var: allowed to change

rating = 4.9              // fine ã rating is a var
// dogName = "Rex"        // compile error! dogName is a val
steps.caption: The compiler refuses to build code that reassigns a `val`. That's a feature, not a punishment.

steps.q: You wrote `val walkMinutes = 30`, and three lines later you write `walkMinutes = 60`. What happens?
choices: The compiler refuses to build ã a `val` can never be reassigned
walkMinutes becomes 60
The app builds but crashes when it runs
Kotlin silently keeps 30 and ignores the new value
steps.explain: A `val` is a promise to the compiler that the reference never changes. Break the promise and the code simply won't compile ã the fix is either to not reassign it, or to declare it with `var`.
steps.nudge: Which keyword means "this can never be reassigned"?

md: ## Types
Every value in Kotlin has a **type** ã the kind of thing it is. Four types cover most of PawWalk:

- **`Int`** ã a whole number: `1500`, `0`, `-3`. Every price in PawWalk is an `Int`.
- **`Double`** ã a number with a decimal point: `4.8`, `0.5`. Walker ratings are `Double`s.
- **`Boolean`** ã exactly `true` or `false`, nothing else.
- **`String`** ã text, wrapped in double quotes: `"Mochi"`.

You usually don't write the type yourself. Kotlin **infers** it from the value on the right of the `=`: `val priceCents = 1500` makes an `Int` (whole number), `val rating = 4.8` makes a `Double` (decimal point).
When you want to spell it out ã or there's no value yet ã add a **type annotation**: a colon and the type after the name, `val priceCents: Int = 1500`. Read the colon as *"of type"*.
One naming convention to lock in now: Kotlin names use **camelCase** ã first word lowercase, every following word capitalized, no spaces or underscores: `dogName`, `pricePer30MinCents`.

steps.title: data/Models.kt ã real PawWalk properties (excerpt)
steps.source:
val name: String
val rating: Double
val pricePer30MinCents: Int
steps.caption: Three lines straight out of the app's Walker type. (They live inside a `data class` ã you'll meet data classes in the next module.) With no `= value` on the line, the type annotation is required: name, colon, type.

steps.q: Kotlin sees `val rating = 4.8`. What type does it infer for `rating`?
choices: Int
String
Double
It can't tell without an annotation
steps.explain: The decimal point is the giveaway: `4.8` is a `Double`, so `rating` is a `Double`. Inference means the annotation is optional whenever there's a value to look at.
steps.nudge: Look at the value on the right of the `=` ã does it have a decimal point?

1. a **val** named `dogName` holding the text `"Mochi"`
2. a **val** named `priceCents` holding the whole number `1500`
3. a **var** named `rating` holding `4.8` ã ratings move as new reviews arrive
Let Kotlin infer all three types ã no annotations needed.

// your code here
steps.solution:
val dogName = "Mochi"
val priceCents = 1500
var rating = 4.8

checks.hint: A dog's name never changes mid-walk ã declare it with `val`, and remember text needs double quotes.
`priceCents` is a val too: `val`, then the bare number 1500 ã no quotes around numbers.
The rating needs to change over time. Which keyword declares a name you can reassign?

mustNot.hint: The name won't change ã Kotlin style says use `val` unless it must be a `var`.
A `val` rating could never be updated when a new review lands. Use `var` for this one.

steps.success: That's the core habit: `val` by default, `var` only when the value truly changes ã and Kotlin inferred String, Int, and Double for you.

md: ## Text, and how to build it
A **String** is text: characters between double quotes, like `"Mochi"`. PawWalk is full of them ã dog names, walker bios, neighborhood names, error messages.

The single most-used String feature in Kotlin is the **string template**: put `$name` inside the quotes, and Kotlin drops that value into the text right there. For anything more than a bare name ã a calculation, a property access ã wrap it in braces: `${expr}`.
You will type `$name` constantly in this course. It's how almost every label in the PawWalk UI gets its text.

steps.title: String templates
steps.source:
val dogName = "Mochi"
val minutes = 30

val greeting = "Time to walk $dogName!"                // "Time to walk Mochi!"
val plan = "A $minutes-minute walk"                    // "A 30-minute walk"
val longer = "Or go big: ${minutes * 2} minutes"  // "Or go big: 60 minutes"
steps.caption: A bare name after `$` is enough. An expression ã like `minutes * 2` ã needs braces: `${minutes * 2}`. Kotlin evaluates what's inside, turns the result into text, and splices it in.

steps.title: data/Models.kt ã Walker.priceLabel
steps.source: val priceLabel: String get() = "$%.0f / 30 min".format(pricePer30MinCents / 100.0)
steps.caption: Real shipping code. `.format(...)` is a String method that fills in a `%.0f` placeholder ã a whole-number-looking decimal ã with the dollar amount, producing text like "$15 / 30 min". (The `val ãÎ get() = ãÎ` wrapper is a *computed property* ã you'll build one yourself in a later module.)

steps.q: You write `val price = 20; val label = "$$price"`. What does `label` hold?
choices: A compile error ã two dollar signs in a row aren't allowed
steps.explain: The first `$` starts the template and interpolates `price` as `20`; the *second* `$` is a plain, literal dollar sign that isn't followed by a valid template start, so it prints as-is. Together: "$20".
steps.nudge: Only a `$` immediately followed by a name or `{` starts a template ã otherwise it's just a literal character.

md: ## Three more string tricks
**Concatenation** ã `+` glues two strings together: `"Paw" + "Walk"` is `"PawWalk"`. Fine for simple gluing; for anything with values in the middle, a template reads better.
**Methods** ã a method is an ability a value carries around, called with a dot. Strings come with lots of them: `dogName.uppercase()` gives `"MOCHI"`. The parentheses mean *do it now*.

**Raw strings** ã three double quotes `"""` wrapping text let a string span multiple lines, line breaks included, with no need to escape most characters. Handy for long text; you'll rarely need it, but you should recognize it.

steps.title: Concatenation, a method, and a raw string
steps.source:
val first = "Paw"
val second = "Walk"
val brand = first + second       // "PawWalk"
val shout = brand.uppercase()    // "PAWWALK"

val poster = """
PawWalk
Walks your dog. Tracks the walk.
"""

steps.title: Build a walk banner
prompt: The app wants a banner line for the booking screen. Using the two vals already in the editor, declare a **val** named `banner` that uses string templates to read exactly:
`Bella walks for 30 minutes`
Use `$dogName` and `$minutes` ã don't retype the word Bella or the number 30.
steps.solution:
val dogName = "Bella"
val minutes = 30
val banner = "$dogName walks for $minutes minutes"

checks.hint: Declare it with `val banner = ãÎ` ã the banner never changes once built.
Start the string with the dog's name templated in: `"$dogName walks for ãÎ"`.
Template the number too: `$minutes` followed by the word minutes.

mustNot.hint: Don't hard-code "Bella" into the string ã template `$dogName` so the banner works for every dog.

steps.success: That's the exact pattern behind nearly every label in PawWalk ã a String built with `$name` templates.

md: ## Int arithmetic
`Int`s support the arithmetic you expect: `+`, `-`, `*` (multiply), `/` (divide). `1500 * 2` is `3000`, `1500 + 500` is `2000`.

Division has a twist worth burning into memory: **when both sides are `Int`s, the result is an `Int` ã the remainder is thrown away.** `7 / 2` is `3`, not `3.5`. There's no rounding: `2599 / 100` is `25`, even though 25.99 is "closer" to 26. This is called **truncation**.
Truncation sounds like a bug waiting to happen. Used deliberately, it's a tool ã and PawWalk uses it on purpose, as you're about to see.

steps.title: Integer division truncates
steps.source:
val doubleWalk = 1500 * 2   // 3000
val half = 7 / 2            // 3   ã the .5 is dropped
val dollars = 2599 / 100    // 25  ã not 25.99, not 26

steps.q: In Kotlin, what is `2599 / 100`?
choices: A compile error
steps.explain: Int divided by Int is an Int: Kotlin keeps the whole part (25) and drops the remainder. No rounding ever happens ã 25.99 truncates to 25.
steps.nudge: Both numbers are Ints, so the answer must be an Int. Does Kotlin round up, or chop?

md: ## Why PawWalk stores money as Int cents

`Double` seems like the natural type for money ã until you learn that computers store `Double`s in binary, and many decimal fractions can't be represented exactly. In Kotlin, `0.1 + 0.2` is *not* `0.3` ã it's `0.30000000000000004`. Tiny errors like that compound, and in a payments app "tiny errors" means *wrong charges*.

The industry fix is simple: **store money as a whole number of cents, in an `Int`.** $15.00 is `1500`. Int math is exact ã no drift, ever. That's why `data/Models.kt` has `pricePer30MinCents: Int` on `Walker` and `priceCents: Int` on `Booking`, and why the backend's JSON sends `price_per_30min_cents`.
Only when a human needs to *read* the price does the app convert cents to dollars ã with integer division, right inside a string template.

steps.title: data/Models.kt ã Booking (excerpt)
steps.source:
val priceCents: Int
val isActive: Boolean get() = status == "pending" || status == "confirmed" || status == "in_progress"
steps.caption: The stored truth is `priceCents` (exact). `isActive` is a different kind of computed property ã a `Boolean` derived from `status` ã but the same idea: compute the friendly view on demand from the exact stored value.

md: ## Converting between Int and Double
Kotlin **never converts number types silently** ã adding an `Int` to a `Double` is a compile error until you convert one of them yourself. You convert with a method named after the target type:

- `priceCents.toDouble()` ã makes `1500.0` from `1500`.
- `4.9.toInt()` ã makes `4`. Same rule as division: **truncation, not rounding.** The decimal part is simply dropped.
> This strictness is on purpose: every conversion that could lose information has to be visible in the code, written by you.

steps.q: What does `4.9.toInt()` give you?
choices: 5 ã it rounds to the nearest whole number
4 ã it truncates, dropping the decimal part
4.9 ã the value is unchanged
A compile error
steps.explain: Converting Double to Int chops off everything after the decimal point ã 4.9 becomes 4. If you ever want real rounding, you have to ask for it explicitly.
steps.nudge: Same behavior as integer division: chop, don't round.

steps.title: Cents to dollars, the PawWalk way
prompt: A booking costs `2599` cents. Add two vals:

1. `dollars` ã the whole-dollar amount, computed from `priceCents` with integer division
2. `priceLabel` ã a String template that reads `$25`
Stay in `Int` the whole way ã no `Double` anywhere.
steps.starter:
val priceCents = 2599
// your code here
steps.solution:
val priceCents = 2599
val dollars = priceCents / 100
val priceLabel = "$$dollars"

checks.hint: Divide the cents by 100: `priceCents / 100`. Truncation does the rest.
Build the label with a template: a literal `$` inside the quotes, then `$dollars`.

mustNot.hint: No Double needed ã integer division already gives you whole dollars, exactly.
Keep everything as whole-number Ints. Decimal points and money don't mix in PawWalk.

steps.success: You just re-derived a PawWalk money label ã exact Int cents in storage, pretty String on demand.

md: ## A named recipe
A **function** is a named chunk of code you can run whenever you want. You give it inputs, it hands back an output. Define once, use everywhere ã the opposite of copy-paste.
The anatomy, left to right:

- **`fun`** ã the keyword that starts every function definition.
- **the name** ã camelCase, usually a verb-ish phrase: `walkPrice`.
- **parameters** in parentheses ã the inputs, each written `name: Type`, separated by commas.
- **`:` and a return type** ã after the parentheses, a colon announces what type the function hands back.
- **the body** in `{ }` ã the code that runs. **`return`** followed by a value ends the function and hands that value back.
Calling a function means writing its name with real values in the parentheses. The call is an expression ã it *becomes* the returned value, so you can assign it straight to a `val`.

steps.title: Define it once, call it anywhere
steps.caption: Two Ints in, one Int out. The call repeats the parameter names ã that's optional in Kotlin, but it reads like a sentence, so PawWalk code uses it for anything non-obvious.

steps.q: What value does `walkPrice(minutes = 60, ratePer30Cents = 1500)` return?
steps.explain: 60 / 30 is 2 (two half-hours), and 2 * 1500 is 3000 cents ã a $30 hour-long walk.
steps.nudge: Work the body by hand: what's `60 / 30`, and what do you multiply it by?

md: ## Named arguments and defaults

Those names at the call ã `minutes = 60` ã are **named arguments**. Most languages call functions like `login(a, b)` and make you guess what `a` is. Kotlin lets you name the argument at the call site, so it documents itself: `login(email = ..., password = ...)`.

Functions can also give a parameter a **default value** with `= value` in the definition. Callers who are happy with the default just leave that argument out entirely ã and once you skip one, any argument after it needs a name so Kotlin knows which parameter you mean.
Here's a teaser from the file where PawWalk talks to its backend ã real signatures you'll write yourself in a later module:

steps.title: data/PawWalkApi.kt ã real signatures (teaser)
steps.source:
suspend fun login(email: String, password: String): AuthResponse
suspend fun signup(email: String, password: String, name: String, role: String): AuthResponse
suspend fun cancelBooking(id: String): Booking
steps.caption: Read the parts you know: `fun`, named parameters, `:` return type. `AuthResponse` and `Booking` are types PawWalk defines for itself in `data/Models.kt` ã you'll build types like them in the next module. `suspend` handles networking without freezing the app ã also a later module.

steps.q: Given `fun book(minutes: Int = 30): Int`, what happens when you call `book()`?
choices: Compile error ã the minutes argument is required
The function runs with minutes set to 0
The function runs with minutes set to the default, 30
Kotlin asks for the value while the app is running
steps.explain: A default value makes the argument optional at the call site. Leave it out and the default (30) is used; pass `book(minutes = 60)` to override it.
steps.nudge: What's the `= 30` in the definition for?

steps.title: Write your first function
prompt: Write a function named `greeting` that takes one parameter, `dogName` of type `String`, and returns a `String`.
Its body should use the `return` keyword to return the templated text `Time to walk $dogName!` ã so `greeting(dogName = "Rex")` returns `Time to walk Rex!`.
The call at the bottom of the editor is already written for you.
steps.starter:
// your code here

println(greeting(dogName = "Rex"))

checks.hint: The shape is `fun greeting(dogName: String): String { ãÎ }` ã keyword, name, parameter with its type, colon, return type, brace.
The body is one line: `return "ãÎ"` ã template the `dogName` parameter into the text with `$dogName`, and don't forget the exclamation mark.

mustNot.hint: Don't hard-code Rex inside the function ã template `$dogName` so the function greets any dog it's given.

steps.success: Inputs in, String out ã and the call site reads like a sentence: `greeting(dogName = "Rex")`. That's Kotlin.

md: ## if / else as an expression

Programs constantly choose between paths. **`if`** runs a block of code only when a **condition** ã an expression whose type is `Boolean` ã is `true`. **`else`** provides the block to run when it isn't.
Conditions usually come from **comparison operators**, each producing a `Boolean`:

- `==` equal (two equals signs! a single `=` assigns)
- `!=` not equal
- `<` `>` less / greater than
- `<=` `>=` less / greater than **or equal**

Kotlin's `if` has a superpower Swift's doesn't: it's an **expression**, meaning it can produce a value directly ã `val label = if (rating >= 4.5) "Top-rated" else "Solid"` skips the temporary variable entirely.

steps.title: Choosing a path

steps.q: With `rating` equal to 4.8, what does the code above print?
choices: Top-rated walker
Solid walker
Both lines
Nothing ã 4.8 isn't exactly 4.5
steps.explain: `4.8 >= 4.5` is `true`, so the `if` block runs and the `else` block is skipped. Exactly one of the two branches ever runs.
steps.nudge: Is `4.8 >= 4.5` true or false?

md: ## when ã one value, many branches

When one value can be several specific things, a chain of `if`s gets clumsy. **`when`** compares a value against a list of branches and runs the first one that matches ã Kotlin's version of `switch`, but also usable as an expression.

When `when` is used as an **expression** ã assigned to a `val`, or returned ã the compiler forces it to be **exhaustive**: every possible value must be covered, or the code won't compile. For an open-ended type like `String`, that means adding an **`else ->`** branch to catch everything you didn't list.

> Preview: PawWalk's bookings have a status ã pending, confirmed, in progress, completed, cancelled ã stored as a `String` for now (you'll meet the stricter `enum class BookingStatus` in the next module). The app renders each status with a `when`, and exhaustiveness on the enum version means the compiler *forces* the UI to handle every status. Forget one, and the app won't build.

steps.title: Deciding on a booking status
steps.source:
val status = "confirmed"

val message = when (status) {
    "pending" -> "Waiting for the walker to accept"
    "confirmed" -> "Walk is on!"
    "cancelled" -> "This walk was cancelled"
    else -> "Some other status"
}
steps.caption: `message` becomes "Walk is on!". Only the matching branch runs ã no fall-through to the next branch, unlike some other languages' switch.

steps.q: You delete the `else ->` branch from the `when` expression above. What happens?
choices: Nothing ã else was never matching anyway
The app crashes if status is something unlisted
It won't compile ã a `when` expression on a String must cover every possible value
Kotlin adds the else branch back automatically
steps.explain: Exhaustiveness is checked at compile time whenever `when` is used as an expression. A String could be anything, so without `else ->` the branches can't cover every possibility ã the compiler stops you right there.
steps.nudge: Remember the `when`-as-expression superpower: *exhaustive*. Can three branches cover every possible String?

md: ## Repeating with loops

**`for`** runs a block once per item in a collection. A **List** is Kotlin's ordered collection ã values in square brackets via `listOf(...)`, like `listOf("Mochi", "Bella")` (`data/Models.kt` uses one: each walker's `neighborhoods` is a `List<String>`; lists get their full treatment in the next module).
`for` also works over a **range**: `1..5` means the numbers 1 through 5, ends included. Perfect for things like drawing a 5-star rating.
**`while`** repeats *as long as* a condition stays true ã useful when you don't know the count in advance. You'll use it rarely; `for` covers most looping in this course.

steps.title: for over a List, a range, and a while
steps.caption: `dog` and `star` are new vals the loop creates for you, one fresh value per pass ã no `val` keyword needed.

steps.title: Instant-book decision
prompt: PawWalk highlights walkers worth booking on the spot. Below the val already in the editor, write an `if` / `else`:

- if `rating` is **greater than or equal to** `4.5`, print `Book instantly!`
- otherwise, print `Read reviews first`
steps.starter:
val rating = 4.9
// your code here

checks.hint: Start with `if (...)`, comparing `rating` against 4.5 ã greater-than-or-equal is `>=`, in that order ã then an opening `{`.
Inside the if block, print exactly `Book instantly!` ã quotes inside the parentheses.
Add an `else { ãÎ }` branch that prints `Read reviews first`.

mustNot.hint: A single `=` assigns a value. To compare you need `>=` (or `==`).

steps.success: Branching, comparison operators, and println ã you now have everything a program needs to make decisions. Module complete: next stop, going one level deeper into Kotlin.

lessons.title: Nullability: When a Value Might Not Exist

md: ## Sometimes there's just no value

Every pet in PawWalk has a name. But its age? A rescue dog's age is often a guess the owner never entered. And not every walker uploads a profile photo. Kotlin forces you to be honest about missing data: a value that *might not be there* gets a different type.

`Double` is **always** a number. `Double?` ã read it as "nullable Double" ã is *either* a number *or* `null`, Kotlin's word for "nothing here." In the real models file, `Pet.ageYears` is a `Double?` and `Walker.photoUrl` is a `String?` for exactly these reasons.

> The `?` is part of the type. `String` and `String?` are different types, and Kotlin will not let you use a `String?` as if the value were definitely there. That one rule eliminates the #1 crash in most other languages: using a value that doesn't exist.

steps.title: data/Models.kt ã Walker (trimmed)
steps.source:
data class Walker(
    val id: String,
    val name: String,
    val photoUrl: String? = null,   // not every walker uploads one
    val rating: Double,
    val pricePer30MinCents: Int,
    val bio: String = "",
    val neighborhoods: List<String> = emptyList(),
)
steps.caption: `data class` bundles values into a type of your own ã that's the very next lesson. The real file also has `@Serializable`/`@SerialName` annotations (Module 03) and a computed property you'll rebuild soon.

steps.q: `Walker.photoUrl` has type `String?`. What can it hold?
choices: Only a URL string
Only null
Either a string or null
An empty string, but never null
steps.explain: That's the whole idea: `String?` is a box that contains a String *or* nothing. Some walkers uploaded a photo, some didn't ã the type tells the truth.
steps.nudge: The `?` means the value is nullable ã what are the two possibilities?

md: ## Getting the value out
Kotlin won't let you use a `String?` where a `String` is needed ã you have to check it first.

The blunt tool is `!!`, the **not-null assertion**: `photoUrl!!` means "I swear this isn't null." If you're wrong, the app **crashes on the spot** with an NPE. You'll see `!!` in the wild; in this course we treat it as a code smell and almost never use it.

The safe tool is the **safe call** `?.`: reach into a nullable without crashing. `photoUrl?.isEmpty()` asks "*if* there's a URL, is it empty?" When `photoUrl` is null, the whole expression just evaluates to `null` instead of blowing up.

steps.title: ?. on a walker's photo
steps.source:
val photoUrl: String? = null   // this walker never uploaded a photo

val length = photoUrl?.length
println(length)   // null ã the safe call short-circuits instead of crashing
steps.caption: `?.` chains: if the receiver is null, the whole expression is null and nothing after the `?.` ever runs.

md: ## `?:` ã the Elvis operator

**`?:`** (nicknamed the *Elvis operator* ã tilt your head, it's a smiley) plugs the hole: "use this value, or if it's null, use that fallback." `val url = photoUrl ?: "placeholder.png"`. The result is a plain `String`, never null, because the fallback fills the gap.
`?.` and `?:` pair up constantly: `photoUrl?.length ?: 0` reads as "the length if there's a URL, otherwise 0."

steps.title: ?: filling in a default
steps.source:
val photoUrl: String? = null
val url = photoUrl ?: "placeholder.png"
println(url)   // placeholder.png
steps.caption: `url` is a non-nullable `String` now ã the compiler can prove it, because `?:` guarantees a value on both sides.

steps.q: `val n: Int? = null`. What does `n ?: 0` evaluate to?
choices: null
An exception is thrown
steps.explain: `?:` says "use the left side unless it's null, then use the right side." `n` is null, so the whole expression becomes the fallback, `0`.
steps.nudge: Elvis operator: left side if present, right side otherwise. Which side is null here?

md: ## `let` ã run code only when there's a value

The scope function `let` runs a block *only if* the receiver isn't null, and inside the block you get a plain, non-nullable reference (conventionally named `it`). Combine it with `?.`: `photoUrl?.let { url -> ... }` means "if there's a URL, do something with it."
You've already seen this pattern in the wild ã `Pet.ageYears?.let { "$it yrs" }`-style code in the real models file turns a nullable age into a display string, or nothing at all.

steps.title: data/Models.kt ã Pet.subtitle (trimmed)
steps.source:
val subtitle: String get() = listOfNotNull(
    breed.ifBlank { null },
    ageYears?.let { "${it.toInt()} yrs" },
    weightKg?.let { "%.1f kg".format(it) },
).joinToString(" ôñ ")
steps.caption: Three optional pieces ã breed, age, weight ã each becomes either a string or `null` via `?.let`. `listOfNotNull` drops the nulls, `joinToString` glues what's left. Full walkthrough later this module.

steps.title: Unwrap a pet's age
prompt: PawWalk shows a pet's age only when it's known. Below, `maybeAge` is a `Double?` that happens to hold a value.

Use `?.let` to print `Age: ` followed by the value, and `?:` so that when it's null the block instead evaluates the string `"Age unknown"`. One expression: `println(maybeAge?.let { "Age: $it" } ?: "Age unknown")`.
steps.starter:
val maybeAge: Double? = 3.0
// your code here
steps.solution:
val maybeAge: Double? = 3.0
println(maybeAge?.let { "Age: $it" } ?: "Age unknown")

checks.hint: Start with `maybeAge?.let { ãÎ }` ã the safe call runs the block only when there's a value.
Inside the block, build the string `"Age: $it"` ã `it` is the unwrapped value.
After the closing brace, add `?: "Age unknown"` for the null case.

mustNot.hint: No `!!`! It crashes when the value is null ã `?.let` is the safe way.

steps.success: Unwrapped safely. Both paths are handled, and nothing can crash ã that's the nullability mindset.

md: ## Making your own types

`Int`, `String`, `Double` ã those are Kotlin's built-ins. But PawWalk thinks in *walkers*, *pets*, and *bookings*. A **data class** lets you define a type of your own: a named bundle of values, with useful behavior generated for free.
Each `val name: String` inside the parentheses is a **property** ã a piece of data that every value of the type carries. You read one with a dot: `walker.name`.
You've already met the most important data class in the app. Here it is for real:

steps.title: data/Models.kt ã Walker
steps.source:
data class Walker(
    val id: String,
    val name: String,
    val photoUrl: String? = null,
    val rating: Double,
    val pricePer30MinCents: Int,
    val bio: String = "",
    val neighborhoods: List<String> = emptyList(),
) {
    val priceLabel: String get() = "$%.0f / 30 min".format(pricePer30MinCents / 100.0)
}
steps.caption: `List<String>` is a list of strings ã this lesson's next stop. `= emptyList()` and `= null` are default values, so callers can skip them. `priceLabel` is a computed property (below).

md: ## What `data` gives you for free

Marking a class `data` tells the compiler to generate several things from the properties in its constructor, with zero code from you: a readable `toString()`, structural `equals()` (two Walkers with the same properties are equal ã no `===` reference-comparison surprises), a matching `hashCode()`, and **`copy()`** ã build a near-identical value with just the fields you want changed.

steps.title: Creating and copying a Walker
steps.source:
val maya = Walker(
    id = "w1",
    name = "Maya P.",
    rating = 4.9,
    pricePer30MinCents = 2400,
    bio = "Dogs > everything.",
    neighborhoods = listOf("Mission", "Castro"),
)

println(maya.name)     // Maya P.
println(maya.priceLabel)  // $24 / 30 min

val raise = maya.copy(pricePer30MinCents = 2600)   // everything else unchanged
println(raise.pricePer30MinCents)   // 2600
println(maya.pricePer30MinCents)    // 2400 ã the original is untouched
steps.caption: Named arguments (`id = "w1"`) make constructor calls self-documenting and order-independent. `copy()` never mutates the original ã it returns a new value.

steps.q: `val b = a.copy(rating = 5.0)` where `a` is a Walker. What happens to `a.rating`?
choices: It also becomes 5.0 ã a and b share state
It stays whatever it was ã copy() returns an independent new value
It becomes null
The code fails to compile
steps.explain: `copy()` builds a brand-new value with the listed properties changed and everything else carried over ã the original is never touched. That's why it's safe to use everywhere.
steps.nudge: The clue is in the name: copy. What does copying leave behind?

steps.title: Your first data class
prompt: Define a data class called `Dog` with two properties, in this order: `val name: String` and `val breed: String`.
Then create a constant `mochi` ã a Dog named `"Mochi"` of breed `"Shiba Inu"`, using named arguments.
steps.solution:
data class Dog(
    val name: String,
    val breed: String,
)

val mochi = Dog(name = "Mochi", breed = "Shiba Inu")

checks.hint: Start with `data class Dog(` ã properties go inside the parentheses.
First property: `val name: String`.
Second property: `val breed: String`.
Create it with named arguments: `Dog(name = "Mochi", breed = "Shiba Inu")`.

steps.success: That's a custom type and a value of it ã the exact pattern behind Walker, Pet, and Booking.

md: ## Computed properties

A **computed property** stores nothing: it computes a fresh value every time you read it. Write it with `get()` after the type: `val priceLabel: String get() = ...`. You *read* it like data ã `walker.priceLabel`, no parentheses ã but it runs code behind the scenes.

Walker stores its price in cents (`pricePer30MinCents` ã 2400 means $24), the cents pattern from Module 01. `priceLabel` formats that into `$24 / 30 min` for display, using `.format(...)` ã Kotlin's number-formatting helper, borrowed from Java's `String.format`.

steps.title: Rebuild Walker.priceLabel ã the real thing
prompt: The Walkers screen displays a walker's price as `$24 / 30 min`.

Add a computed property ã call it `priceLabel`, type `String` ã using `get() =` whose expression formats `pricePer30MinCents / 100.0` into the text `$ãÎ / 30 min`, matching the real file exactly: `"$%.0f / 30 min".format(pricePer30MinCents / 100.0)`.

checks.hint: Declare it `val priceLabel: String get() = "$%.0f / 30 min".format(ãÎ)` ã a computed property, formatted string.
Pass `pricePer30MinCents / 100.0` to `.format(...)` ã dividing by a `Double` (100.0) keeps the fractional dollars before `%.0f` rounds them.

mustNot.hint: A computed property, not a function ã use `val ãÎ get() = `, no parentheses when reading it.

steps.success: Character for character, that's the real line from Models.kt. You just wrote shipping PawWalk code.

lessons.title: Enums & Sealed Interfaces: A Fixed Set of Choices

md: ## A fixed set of choices

A PawWalk user is an *owner* or a *walker*. Not both, not `"ownerr"` with a typo, not some surprise third thing. When a value can only be one of a known, fixed set of choices, Kotlin gives you the **enum class**.
You declare the choices as **entries**. A value of the type is exactly one entry: `val status = BookingStatus.PENDING`.

steps.title: An enum, a value, a when
steps.caption: `when` is Kotlin's `switch`. No `else` needed here: the compiler can see the `when` covers every entry of the enum, and *verifies* that it does. Add a fourth entry tomorrow and this `when` becomes a compile error until you handle it.

steps.q: You add `EXPRESS` to an exhaustive `when` over an enum (no `else` branch). What happens?
choices: Nothing ã the when just skips unknown entries
The app crashes the first time EXPRESS shows up
The code stops compiling until the when handles EXPRESS
Kotlin adds a branch for you
steps.explain: Exhaustiveness is enforced at compile time for `when` used as a statement or expression over a sealed type. The compiler walks you to every `when` that needs updating.
steps.nudge: The compiler *checks* that a when is exhaustive. What does a checker do when the check fails?

md: ## Sealed interfaces: choices that carry different data

Now the feature that shapes every screen in PawWalk. `PawWalk`'s real `BookingStatus` is plain strings straight off the wire (you'll type it in Module 3). But the Walkers *screen state* needs something richer: it's *loading*, or it *loaded* some walkers, or it *failed* with an error message. Three shapes, and two of them carry different data.

A **sealed interface** declares a closed family of types: every implementation must live in the same file (or module), so the compiler knows the *complete* list ã just like an enum's entries, except each case can be its own type with its own payload. `data object` is for a case with no data (a lightweight singleton); `data class` is for a case that carries something.

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkersViewModel.kt ã UiState
steps.caption: Verbatim from the repo (trimmed to the sealed interface ã `load()` and the StateFlow plumbing are Module 5's topic). `MutableStateFlow`/`StateFlow` are how the ViewModel publishes state; ignore them for now and focus on the three UiState shapes.

steps.title: Switching on UiState
steps.caption: `is X ->` checks which implementation `state` actually is, and ã because `Success` and `Error` are data classes ã Kotlin *smart-casts* inside each branch, so `state.walkers` and `state.message` are already the right type with no unwrapping step.

steps.q: Why must every implementation of a sealed interface live in the same file (or module)?
choices: It's just a style convention with no compiler effect
So the compiler can see the complete list of possibilities and check `when` is exhaustive
To make the file shorter
Sealed interfaces don't have this restriction
steps.explain: That restriction is the whole point: it lets the compiler prove a `when` over the sealed type covers every possibility, the same guarantee an enum gives you ã but with each case free to carry its own data.
steps.nudge: Think about what an exhaustive `when` needs to know in advance.

steps.title: Rebuild UiState
prompt: From memory: declare `sealed interface UiState` with three members ã `data object Loading` (no payload), `data class Success` carrying `val walkers: List<Walker>`, and `data class Error` carrying `val message: String`. Each implements `UiState`, exactly like the real file.
steps.starter:
// Rebuild WalkersViewModel's UiState
// your code here

checks.hint: Start with `sealed interface UiState {`.
The no-payload case is `data object Loading : UiState`.
`Success` carries its payload in its constructor: `data class Success(val walkers: List<Walker>) : UiState`.
`Error` carries a message: `data class Error(val message: String) : UiState`.

steps.success: That's the exact sealed interface from WalkersViewModel.kt. Every screen you build in this course will have a UiState just like it.

md: ## Many values, one name

A walker covers several neighborhoods, an owner has several bookings, the backend returns a *list* of walkers. A **List** is an ordered collection of values that all share one type. The type is written in angle brackets ã `List<String>` is "list of String" ã and a literal is built with `listOf(...)`. That's exactly what `Walker.neighborhoods` is.
Lists come with built-in properties and functions: `.size` (how many), `.isEmpty()` (is it zero?), and `.firstOrNull()` (the first element ã as a *nullable*, because the list might be empty).

steps.title: List basics
steps.source:
val neighborhoods = listOf("Mission", "Castro", "Noe Valley")

println(neighborhoods.size)                      // 3
println(neighborhoods.isEmpty())                 // false
println(neighborhoods.firstOrNull() ?: "Anywhere")  // Mission
steps.caption: `.firstOrNull()` returns `String?` ã Lesson 1's nullability showing up exactly where promised ã so `?:` gives it a fallback.

steps.q: `val none = listOf<String>()` ã what is `none.firstOrNull()`?
choices: An empty string ""
It throws an exception
null ã firstOrNull() is nullable for exactly this case
steps.explain: An empty list has no first element, and Kotlin's honest answer to "give me something that isn't there" is always the same: a nullable holding null.
steps.nudge: What did Lesson 1 say Kotlin does when a value might not exist?

steps.title: Make a list
prompt: Create a constant `dogs` holding the list `listOf("Mochi", "Biscuit", "Rex")`, then print how many dogs there are using `.size`.
steps.starter: // your code here
steps.solution:
val dogs = listOf("Mochi", "Biscuit", "Rex")
println(dogs.size)

checks.hint: `listOf(...)` builds a list from comma-separated values: `listOf("Mochi", "Biscuit", "Rex")`.
`.size` gives the list's length ã print that.

mustNot.hint: `size` is a property, not a function ã no parentheses after it.

steps.success: Three dogs, counted. Now for the fun part: doing things to *every* element at once.

md: ## Lambdas: functions as values
A **lambda** is a function without a name that you can pass around like any other value. Lists love them: "here's my list ã and here's a little function describing what to do with each item."

The workhorse is `map`: transform every element, get a new list back. When a lambda is the *last* argument to a call, Kotlin lets you move it outside the parentheses ã **trailing lambda** syntax ã and when it takes one argument, `it` is shorthand for "the current element." So `prices.map { it / 100 }` reads: *for each price, divide it by 100*.

steps.title: map, filter, sumOf
steps.source:
val prices = listOf(2400, 3000, 1800)                  // cents, as always

val dollars = prices.map { it / 100 }                  // [24, 30, 18]
val affordable = prices.filter { it <= 2500 }          // [2400, 1800]
val total = prices.sumOf { it }                        // 7200
steps.caption: `map` = transform each element. `filter` = keep the elements where the lambda says true. `sumOf` = add up a numeric value derived from each element.

steps.title: Price tags with map
prompt: The Walkers screen needs display prices. Use `map` to create a constant `labels` from `prices`: inside the lambda, first pull the whole-dollar amount into a `val dollars = it / 100`, then build the string `"$dollars"` ã so 2400 becomes `"$24"`. This is the safe `$name` template form: a `val` first, then interpolate its plain name (no braces, no expression inside the string).
steps.starter:
val prices = listOf(2400, 1900, 3100)
// your code here

checks.hint: Trailing lambda syntax: `prices.map { ãÎ }` ã the braces go right after `map`, no parentheses needed.
Inside the lambda, extract a `val dollars = it / 100` first.
Then build the string `"$dollars"` ã the simple `$name` template form, no braces needed.

mustNot.hint: No loop needed ã `map` does the looping for you.

steps.success: ["$24", "$19", "$31"] ã an Int list in, a String list out, using the safe `$name` interpolation form.

steps.q: In `prices.filter { it <= 2500 }`, what is `it`?
choices: Each price, one at a time, as filter walks the list
The first price only
The size of the list
A currency symbol
steps.explain: `it` is the lambda's single implicit argument ã filter calls the lambda once per element, so `it` takes each price in turn.
steps.nudge: The lambda runs once per element. What does it get handed each time?

md: ## listOfNotNull and joinToString

`listOfNotNull` is `listOf`'s null-hating cousin: build a list from the arguments you pass it, silently dropping any that are `null`. Pair it with `?.let` (Lesson 1) and you get a list built entirely from *maybe-there* pieces, with the gaps already gone.
`joinToString(separator = ...)` glues a list of strings into one string, with the separator between elements.

Together they power one of the app's nicest small touches: a pet's subtitle line ã breed, age, weight ã where *any piece might be missing*. Build a list of nullable pieces, drop the nulls, glue what's left. Missing pieces simply vanish; no dangling separators, no `if` pyramid.

steps.title: data/Models.kt ã Pet.subtitle
steps.source:
val subtitle: String get() = listOfNotNull(
    breed.ifBlank { null },
    ageYears?.let { "${it.toInt()} yrs" },
    weightKg?.let { "%.1f kg".format(it) },
).joinToString(" ôñ ")
steps.caption: Verbatim from the repo. `breed.ifBlank { null }` turns an empty breed string into `null` so it's dropped too. The shape to take away: build a `List<String?>` with `listOfNotNull`, then `joinToString` the survivors.

steps.title: A simplified subtitle

md: ## A contract for capabilities
An **interface** is a list of requirements ã "any type that implements me must provide these properties and functions." It says nothing about *how*. It's a contract, not an implementation.

A type **implements** an interface by listing it after a colon (the same spot a class lists its superclass) and supplying whatever the contract demands. PawWalk's repositories are plain Kotlin `object`s (singletons) rather than interface implementations for now ã but the pattern matters everywhere in Android: ViewModels expose `StateFlow`, screens expose `Composable` ã all built on "promise to provide X, and callers can rely on it."

The simplest real example in the app: `WalkerRepository` promises `fetchWalkers()`, `myProfile()`, and `updateProfile()` ã the ViewModel above never needs to know *how* those hit the network, only that they exist.

steps.title: data/WalkerRepository.kt
steps.caption: `object` declares a singleton ã one shared instance, no constructor call needed. `suspend fun` is a function that can pause without blocking a thread (Module 3's topic). The ViewModel from Lesson 3 calls `WalkerRepository.fetchWalkers()` and trusts the contract.

steps.q: What does `object WalkerRepository` give you that `class WalkerRepository` wouldn't, without extra code?
choices: Nothing ã they behave identically
A single shared instance, reachable as `WalkerRepository.fetchWalkers()` with no `WalkerRepository()` construction
Automatic network retries
The ability to have multiple instances
steps.explain: `object` is Kotlin's built-in singleton: the compiler creates exactly one instance and you refer to it by name. No manual singleton boilerplate, unlike older Java patterns.
steps.nudge: Look at how the ViewModel calls it ã is there ever a `WalkerRepository(...)` constructor call?

md: ## When things go wrong, loudly
Networks drop, servers reject, emails are already taken. Kotlin's error handling uses ordinary exceptions: any `Throwable` (usually an `Exception`) can be **thrown** with `throw`.

You handle failure with `try { } catch (e: Exception) { }`: run the `try` block, and if anything inside throws, execution jumps straight to `catch`. Unlike some languages, Kotlin doesn't require you to mark a function as "can throw" ã any function can throw at any time, so a `try/catch` at the boundary (here, the ViewModel) is what keeps failures from crashing the app.
Here's the pattern in the real app ã the function that loads the walkers list, feeding the `UiState` sealed interface you built in Lesson 3:

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkersViewModel.kt ã load()
steps.caption: `viewModelScope.launch { }` is Module 3's big topic ã for now, read it as "run this in the background." Everything else here is yours already: try/catch, and `_state.value` flipping between the UiState cases.

steps.q: In `load()`, the backend is down and `fetchWalkers()` throws. Which line runs next?
choices: `UiState.Success` with an empty list
The app crashes
`UiState.Error(...)` inside the catch block
Nothing ã the exception is silently ignored
steps.explain: The throw skips the rest of the `try` block and lands in `catch`, so `Success` never happens ã the screen shows the error state instead. Failure is a *state*, not a crash.
steps.nudge: What is `catch` for?

md: ## try as an expression

One Kotlin idiom worth calling out: `try/catch` isn't just a statement, it's an **expression** ã it produces a value, just like `if`. That's how `load()` assigns `_state.value = try { ... } catch (e: Exception) { ... }` directly: whichever branch runs, its last expression becomes the value assigned.
This is the same "everything is an expression" spirit as `if`/`else` and `when` in Kotlin ã one reason Kotlin code tends to have fewer temporary `var`s than you'd expect.

modules.title: Kotlin for Real Apps: Serialization & Coroutines

md: ## The language your app and backend share
PawWalk's Android app and its Python backend are two separate programs. They agree on exactly one thing: the shape of the **JSON** they send each other.

JSON (*JavaScript Object Notation*) is plain text for structured data. An object is `{ }` with `"key": value` pairs inside; values can be strings, numbers, `true`/`false`, `null`, arrays `[ ]`, or nested objects. That's the whole format.
Here's a real walker, exactly as the backend sends it when the app asks for the walkers list:

steps.title: docs/API-CONTRACT.md ã one Walker, as GET /walkers sends it
steps.source:
{
  "id": "wlk_123",
  "name": "Sam Rivera",
  "photo_url": "https://ãÎ",
  "rating": 4.9,
  "price_per_30min_cents": 1800,
  "bio": "10 yrs with dogs. Loves huskies.",
  "neighborhoods": ["Mission", "SoMa"]
}
steps.caption: Every field the Walkers screen shows is right here ã the app's job is to turn this text into a Kotlin value it can work with.

steps.q: In the JSON above, `price_per_30min_cents` is 1800. Which Kotlin type should hold it?
choices: Double ã money has decimals
Int ã the contract says money is always whole cents
String ã everything in JSON is text
Boolean
steps.explain: PawWalk's API contract is strict: money is always integer cents, never floats ã 1800 cents is $18.00. The client formats it for display (that's what `priceLabel` is for).
steps.nudge: Check the field name again ã what unit is the price already in?

md: ## kotlinx.serialization: the bridge

`@Serializable` is an annotation ã you stick it on a `data class` and a compiler plugin **writes the JSON conversion code for you**. Both directions: JSON ã Kotlin is *deserializing*, Kotlin ã JSON is *serializing*.
Two new pieces make decoding work:

- **`Json`** ã the object that does the converting. You call `Json.decodeFromString<T>(text)`, where `T` is the type you want back.
- **The type parameter (`<Walker>` or `<List<Walker>>`)** ã this is how you tell `decodeFromString` *what shape* to expect. Kotlin's generics let one function work for any `@Serializable` type.

Unlike Swift's `try`, a bad decode in Kotlin throws an ordinary exception (`SerializationException`) ã you'll catch those with `try`/`catch` later this module. For now, assume the JSON is well-formed and focus on the shape.

steps.title: Decoding the walkers list
steps.source:
import kotlinx.serialization.Serializable
import kotlinx.serialization.json.Json

@Serializable
data class Walker(
    val id: String,
    val name: String,
    val rating: Double,
    val bio: String,
    val neighborhoods: List<String>,
)

// json: String ã the GET /walkers response body, arrived from the network as
// raw text: a JSON array [ {ãÎ}, {ãÎ} ] of walkers like the one above
val walkers = Json.decodeFromString<List<Walker>>(json)
println(walkers[0].name)   // "Sam Rivera"
steps.caption: A first cut of Walker ã only the fields whose names already match the JSON exactly. photoUrl and pricePer30MinCents need a trick you'll learn next lesson.

steps.q: Why does `Walker` need the `@Serializable` annotation at all?
choices: It doesn't ã any data class can be decoded
It tells the kotlinx.serialization compiler plugin to generate the JSON conversion code for this class
It makes the class faster
It's required for every Kotlin class, not just data classes
steps.explain: Without `@Serializable`, `Json.decodeFromString` has no generated code to call ã the annotation is what triggers the compiler plugin to write the (de)serializer for that exact class shape.
steps.nudge: Think about what's different between a plain `data class` and one Json.decodeFromString actually works on.

1. Define a `data class Health` marked `@Serializable`, with two `String` properties: `status` and `version` (in that order).
2. Then decode it: a `val` named `health`, using `Json.decodeFromString<Health>(...)`. The text is already in a `val` called `json`.

checks.hint: Mark it `@Serializable` on the line above `data class Health(` ã that's what unlocks decoding.
Give the data class two `String` properties ã `status` first, then `version`, each as `val`.
Pass the type as a generic parameter ã `Json.decodeFromString<Health>(json)` ã no `.self`, that's a Swift thing.

mustNot.hint: This JSON is a single object `{ãÎ}`, not an array ã decode `<Health>`, not `<List<Health>>`.

steps.success: You just did what PawWalk does with every response from the backend: text in, typed Kotlin value out.

md: ## Two naming worlds collide

The backend is Python, and Python names things in **snake_case**: `photo_url`, `price_per_30min_cents`. Kotlin names things in **camelCase**: `photoUrl`, `pricePer30MinCents`. Neither side should bend ã each should look idiomatic in its own language.

But by default, kotlinx.serialization matches property names to JSON keys **character for character**. So `val photoUrl: String?` goes looking for a JSON key literally called `photoUrl` ã which isn't there. Because it's nullable, decoding doesn't even fail: `photoUrl` just comes back `null`, *silently*, and every walker photo in the app is blank. `val pricePer30MinCents: Int` is worse-but-honest: it's not nullable and has no default, so decoding **throws**.
## The fix: @SerialName

You annotate the property itself ã no separate enum needed, unlike Swift's `CodingKeys`. `@SerialName("photo_url") val photoUrl: String? = null` tells the plugin: read the JSON key `photo_url`, store it in the Kotlin property `photoUrl`. Properties whose name already matches the JSON key (`id`, `name`, `rating`) need no annotation at all.

steps.title: data/Models.kt
steps.caption: The real, shipping Walker. `priceLabel` is a computed property like the ones you wrote in Module 02 ã and the default values (`= null`, `= ""`, `= emptyList()`) mean a missing key doesn't throw, it just falls back.

steps.q: In `@SerialName("photo_url") val photoUrl: String? = null`, what does the string inside the annotation name?
choices: The Kotlin property
The exact JSON key the backend sends
A default value used when the key is missing
The type of the field
steps.explain: The annotation's argument is the backend's JSON key, verbatim. The property name after `val` is the idiomatic Kotlin name. The decoder reads `photo_url` from the JSON and stores it in `photoUrl`.
steps.nudge: One name belongs to Kotlin, the other belongs to the wire. Which one is inside the parentheses?

steps.q: You add `val bio: String` to Walker (no default value, no @SerialName) and the backend's JSON omits `bio` entirely for one walker. What happens?
choices: It decodes fine ã `bio` is just an empty string
Decoding throws ã a non-nullable property with no default and a missing key is an error
The property is silently set to the string "null"
kotlinx.serialization automatically adds a default of ""
steps.explain: Unlike a nullable property (which silently becomes `null` when a key is missing), a non-optional property with no default value has nowhere to get a value from ã decoding throws a `MissingFieldException`. That's why the real `Walker` gives `bio` a default of `""`.
steps.nudge: Compare this to `photoUrl: String?` ã that one has an escape hatch. Does plain `String` have the same one?

    val name: String,

    val rating: Double,

    val bio: String = "",
    val neighborhoods: List<String> = emptyList(),

checks.hint: Put `@SerialName("photo_url")` directly before `val photoUrl: String? = null`.
Put `@SerialName("price_per_30min_cents")` directly before `val pricePer30MinCents: Int` ã every underscore counts.

mustNot.hint: The annotation argument is the backend's snake_case key (`photo_url`), not the Kotlin property name.

steps.success: Character for character, that's the annotations shipping in Models.kt. Photos load, prices decode, and neither Python nor Kotlin had to bend.

md: ## One data class per JSON shape

`data/Models.kt` is the whole API contract, translated into Kotlin: one `data class` for every JSON shape in `docs/API-CONTRACT.md`. It also gives some of them a computed property, the same trick from Module 02 ã logic derived from stored fields, at zero storage cost.

PawWalk's Android app keeps `status` as a plain `String` (not an enum) so an unexpected backend value never crashes decoding ã but the contract still treats it as a closed set, and `Booking` exposes a computed `Boolean` so the rest of the app never has to compare strings by hand.

steps.title: data/Models.kt
steps.caption: Eight properties, five of them remapped with @SerialName, plus one computed property ã the exact shape shipping in the app.

steps.q: `Booking.status` is `val status: String`, with no default value and no `?`. What does that tell you about the `status` key in the JSON?
choices: It's optional ã the backend might omit it
It must always be present, or decoding this Booking throws
It defaults to "pending" automatically
Kotlin ignores this property entirely
steps.explain: No `?` and no `= default` means this property has exactly one way to get a value: the JSON key must be there. That's a deliberate choice ã a booking with no status would be meaningless, so the contract makes it required.
steps.nudge: Compare `status: String` to `bio: String = ""` on Walker ã one of them has an escape hatch if the key is missing.

steps.q: The backend adds a new field, `"tip_cents": 200`, to Booking JSON. Your Booking data class doesn't mention it. What happens when the app decodes a booking?
choices: Decoding throws ã the JSON no longer matches
The app crashes on launch
Nothing ã decoding ignores JSON keys your data class doesn't ask for
Kotlin automatically adds a `tipCents` property
steps.explain: Extra keys in the JSON are simply skipped ã that's what lets the backend grow without breaking old app versions. The reverse is the dangerous direction: a non-nullable property with no default whose key goes *missing* makes decoding throw.
steps.nudge: One direction of mismatch is forgiving, the other throws. Which is which?

(Skip `createdAt` ã just end the list after `priceCents`.)

md: ## The main thread

Everything your user sees runs on one lane of execution called the **main thread**. It redraws the screen dozens of times a second, and it handles every tap, scroll, and animation. It's fast ã as long as nobody makes it *wait*.

A network request to the PawWalk backend takes anywhere from 50 milliseconds to several seconds on hotel Wi-Fi. If the main thread stood still waiting for that response, the app would **freeze**: scrolling dies mid-flick, buttons ignore taps, spinners stop spinning. Users experience it as "the app is broken" ã and Android will eventually show an *Application Not Responding* dialog and offer to kill it.
So the rule is: the main thread may *start* slow work, but it must never *stand around* waiting for it.

steps.q: The walkers list takes 2 seconds to download and the main thread waits, doing nothing else. What does the user see during those 2 seconds?
choices: A loading spinner, animating smoothly
A completely frozen app ã no scrolling, no taps, spinner stuck
The previous screen, fully interactive
Nothing changes; downloads happen on a separate device
steps.explain: Even the loading spinner is drawn by the main thread ã if it's stuck waiting, nothing animates and nothing responds. That's exactly the disaster coroutines exist to prevent.
steps.nudge: Who draws the spinner?

md: ## suspend fun and coroutines
Kotlin's answer is a **coroutine** ã a chunk of work that can *pause* itself without blocking the thread it's running on. Two pieces make this work:

- **`suspend`** in front of `fun` marks it as *pausable*: `suspend fun fetchWalkers(): List<Walker>`. It can only be called from inside another `suspend` function or a coroutine ã the compiler enforces this so you can never accidentally call slow code from the main thread directly.
- **`launch { ãÎ }`** starts a new coroutine from ordinary, non-suspend code ã the bridge in, much like Swift's `Task { ãÎ }`. Inside the braces you can freely call `suspend` functions.

If you've compared notes with the iOS track: `suspend fun` is Kotlin's `async`, and calling a suspend function is like Swift's `await` ã except Kotlin needs no extra keyword at the call site, just the fact that you're inside a coroutine or another `suspend fun`.
Here's the real thing, from `WalkerRepository.kt`:

steps.title: data/WalkerRepository.kt
steps.caption: Every function here is `suspend` ã none of them freeze the thread they're called from, they just pause until the network answers.

steps.q: Why must `fetchWalkers()` be marked `suspend`?
choices: It's a style convention with no real effect
It calls `api.getWalkers()`, a network call that needs to pause without blocking the thread ã and the compiler requires suspend functions to only be called from other suspend code
`suspend` makes the function run faster
Every function inside an `object` must be `suspend`
steps.explain: `suspend` isn't decoration ã it's how the compiler tracks which functions can pause. Since `fetchWalkers` awaits a network call, it must itself be `suspend`, which is exactly what forces every caller up the chain to also be a coroutine or another suspend function.
steps.nudge: What would happen if a plain, non-suspend function tried to directly wait on a network response?

md: ## viewModelScope: the ignition key

One catch: you can only call a `suspend` function from inside a coroutine, but the code that *starts* things ã a screen appearing, a button tap ã is ordinary non-suspend code. The bridge in a `ViewModel` is **`viewModelScope.launch { ãÎ }`**: it starts a new coroutine tied to that screen's lifetime, and Android automatically cancels it if the screen goes away before the work finishes ã no manual cleanup, no leaked network calls.

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkersViewModel.kt
steps.caption: `load()` itself is a normal, non-suspend function ã it just opens a coroutine with `launch` and does the suspending work inside. `UiState` and `try`/`catch` both return to Module 04 and Module 05 in full.

2. Below it, start the work from non-suspend code: `viewModelScope.launch { }`, calling `loadWalkers()`, storing the result in a `val` named `walkers`, and printing `walkers.size`.

Wrap the kickoff in `viewModelScope.launch { ãÎ }` ã that's how non-suspend code starts coroutine work in a ViewModel.
Inside the launch block: `val walkers = loadWalkers()` ã no extra keyword needed at the call site.

md: ## Functions that draw the screen

You've spent three modules on plain Kotlin ã no screen, no pixels. Time to fix that. Jetpack Compose's one big idea: **a UI building block is just a function**, marked with an annotation that tells Compose "this function describes some UI."

That annotation is `@Composable`. Slap it on a function, and instead of returning a value, the function's body *emits* UI by calling other `@Composable` functions inside it ã `Text`, `Row`, `Button`, and eventually your own. Compose calls your function to find out what to draw, and calls it again ã **recomposition** ã whenever the state it reads changes.

> If you've done the iOS track: a `@Composable` function is Kotlin's answer to a SwiftUI `View` struct. Different spelling (`fun` vs `struct` + `body`), same declarative idea ã describe the screen for the current state, don't poke pixels by hand.

steps.title: Your first composable
steps.caption: Five lines. An import, an annotation, a function declaration, and one call to another composable ã that's the whole recipe.

md: ## Reading it line by line

- `import androidx.compose.material3.Text` ã Compose's UI pieces live in packages you import, same as any other Kotlin class or function.
- `@Composable fun WelcomeBanner()` ã a **composable function**: an ordinary Kotlin `fun`, just annotated. It takes no parameters and returns `Unit` (nothing) ã its whole job is emitting UI as a side effect of running.
- `Text("Welcome to PawWalk Þƒ")` ã `Text` is itself a `@Composable` function from Material 3. Calling a composable from inside another composable is how you compose (hence the name) bigger UI out of smaller pieces.
## @Preview: see it without running the app
Compiling and launching the emulator for every tiny change would be painfully slow. Android Studio's `@Preview` annotation renders a composable **right in the editor**, no emulator needed:

It's marked with the `@Composable` annotation
It's named starting with a capital letter

steps.explain: The `@Composable` annotation is the whole signal ã it tells the Compose compiler this function describes UI and may call other composables. Naming with a capital letter is just a convention people follow, not a rule the compiler enforces.
