md: ## The scrolling workhorse

Almost every content screen in PawWalk is a list on display: walkers, bookings, pets. Compose's tool for that is **LazyColumn** ã a vertically scrolling list that only composes the rows currently visible on screen, plus a small buffer. Scroll a thousand-walker list and it stays fast, because it never builds all thousand rows at once.

Contrast that with plain `Column`, which you met in Module 04: a `Column` lays out *everything* you hand it immediately, whether it's on screen or not. Fine for a handful of fixed items ã a real problem for a list that could hold hundreds. `LazyColumn` is `Column`'s lazy cousin, built for exactly that case.

Inside a `LazyColumn`, you don't put children directly ã you call `items(...)` inside a trailing lambda that acts as a small DSL (`LazyListScope`). `items(walkers) { walker -> WalkerCard(walker) }` says: for each element of `walkers`, run this closure and place the result as one row.

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkersScreen.kt ã the loaded state
steps.caption: `verticalArrangement = Arrangement.spacedBy(12.dp)` puts 12dp of breathing room between every row ã no per-row bottom padding needed. `items` needs an `import androidx.compose.foundation.lazy.items` alongside `LazyColumn`'s own import.

steps.q: A plain `Column` and a `LazyColumn` can both display a list of items. What's the real difference?
choices: LazyColumn only composes the rows currently visible on screen; Column builds everything up front
LazyColumn is only for text, Column is for anything else
They're identical ã LazyColumn is just a newer name
Column can scroll automatically; LazyColumn cannot scroll at all
steps.explain: That's the whole point of the word "lazy": rows are composed on demand as they scroll into view, so a 1,000-walker list costs about the same as a 10-walker one.
steps.nudge: Which one would choke if you handed it a thousand items ã and why?

md: ## Why `key` matters

Look closely and `WalkersScreen.kt` calls `items(s.walkers) { walker -> ãÎ }` with **no key** ã Compose falls back to using each item's position in the list as its identity. That's fine here because the walkers list is loaded once and never reordered underneath the user.

But `WalkerScreen.kt`'s assigned-walks list writes `items(state.bookings, key = { it.id })` ã an explicit key. Bookings *do* change shape while the screen is open: a walker taps **Accept**, and that booking's status flips from `pending` to `confirmed`, possibly reordering the list. Without a stable key, Compose can only compare "the item that used to be at position 2" to "the item now at position 2" ã and if the list reordered, it may reuse the wrong row's internal state (scroll position, remembered UI) for the wrong booking.

**The rule of thumb:** if a lazy list's items can be added, removed, or reordered while the screen is alive, pass `key = { it.id }` (or whatever uniquely identifies an item). If the list is loaded once and stays put, the default is harmless ã but reaching for `key` out of habit costs nothing and saves you a subtle bug later.

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkerScreen.kt ã a keyed list
steps.caption: `it.id` is the booking's unique id from the backend ã the same id used everywhere in `docs/API-CONTRACT.md`. `WalkCard` is the row; `viewModel.act(b.id, action)` posts an accept/decline/start/complete action for that one booking.

steps.q: A walker accepts one booking out of five, which re-sorts the list by status. Which version handles that re-sort more safely ã `items(bookings) { ãÎ }` or `items(bookings, key = { it.id }) { ãÎ }`?
choices: They behave identically either way
The unkeyed version ã keys only matter for LazyRow, not LazyColumn
The keyed version ã Compose tracks each row by booking id instead of by position, so state follows the right item
Neither ã LazyColumn always re-fetches from scratch on any change
steps.explain: A stable key tells Compose "this row IS booking abc123," no matter where it lands after a reorder. Position-based identity (the default) can silently mix up per-row state when the order changes.

lessons.title: Navigation Compose: Routes & the Back Stack

md: ## The Android way to move between screens

You've seen individual screens ã `WalkersScreen`, `WalkerScreen`, `HomeScreen` ã but nothing yet about *moving between them*. Android's standard answer is a library called **Navigation Compose**. Three pieces:

- **`NavController`** ã an object that remembers which screen is showing and the stack of screens behind it. You get one with `val navController = rememberNavController()`.
- **`NavHost`** ã a composable that owns a `NavController` and a map of **routes**: string ids ãÎ which composable to show. `NavHost(navController, startDestination = "walkers") { composable("walkers") { WalkersScreen(...) } }`.
- **Routes** ã plain strings, like `"walkers"` or `"walker/{walkerId}"`. Navigating is `navController.navigate("walker/w1")`; going back is `navController.popBackStack()` (or the system Back button, which does the same thing automatically).
This is the standard, most-taught way to navigate in a Compose app ã and worth knowing well, because you'll meet it in almost every tutorial and most production codebases.

steps.title: A NavHost, in general Compose form
steps.caption: `"walker/{walkerId}"` is a route *template* ã the `{walkerId}` segment is a placeholder that Navigation Compose fills in from whatever you pass to `navigate("walker/w1")`, then hands back to you as an argument on the destination side.

steps.q: In the route template `"walker/{walkerId}"`, what does `{walkerId}` mean?
choices: A literal path segment ã the route only matches the exact string "{walkerId}"
A placeholder argument ã Navigation Compose fills it in from the value passed to navigate() and hands it back on the destination side
A comment that Navigation Compose ignores
The name of the composable function to call
steps.explain: Curly braces mark a route argument. navigate("walker/w1") matches the template with walkerId = "w1", and the destination composable reads it back off the backStackEntry.
steps.nudge: What changes between navigate("walker/w1") and navigate("walker/w2")?

md: ## How PawWalk actually does it ã and why

Now the honest part: open `MainActivity.kt` and you will **not** find a `NavHost`. PawWalk's whole post-login flow is one hand-rolled `sealed interface Screen` plus Compose's `Crossfade`, switched on with a plain `when`. There's even a comment admitting the tradeoff, right there in the file:

`// ponytail: state-based nav instead of Navigation Compose ã one screen
// stack is plenty for this flow, add a real nav graph if it grows.`

That's a deliberate, size-appropriate choice, not a mistake: PawWalk's whole app is a handful of screens with no deep drill-down and no back-stack sharing across tabs, so a `var screen by remember { mutableStateOf<Screen>(Screen.Home) }` plus a `when` does the job in far less code than wiring up `NavHost` and route strings. Navigation Compose earns its keep once you have many destinations, deep links, or a back stack that needs to survive process death ã none of which PawWalk needs yet. You now know **both** tools: the industry-standard one (Navigation Compose, above) and the pragmatic one PawWalk actually ships (next).

steps.title: MainActivity.kt ã the real navigation model
steps.source:
private sealed interface Screen {
    data object Home : Screen
    data class Live(val bookingId: String? = null) : Screen
    data object Walkers : Screen
    data class CreateBooking(val walker: Walker) : Screen
    data object Bookings : Screen
    data object Profile : Screen
    data object Pets : Screen
    data object Assistant : Screen
}
steps.caption: A `sealed interface` (Module 03) lists every possible screen as a `data object` (no data) or `data class` (carries data ã `CreateBooking` needs to know *which* walker). Read the type as "one of these eight things, exhaustively, nothing else."

steps.title: MainActivity.kt ã switching on it
steps.caption: Trimmed for the lesson (see the real file for all eight cases). `remember { mutableStateOf(...) }` is Module 05's tool ã `screen` is just state. `Crossfade` fades between whatever the state currently is, which is the entire animation budget this navigation model needs. "Going back" is nothing special: a screen's `onClose` just assigns `screen` back to wherever it came from.

steps.q: In PawWalk's `Screen` model, how does `WalkersScreen` hand off to the booking screen with the tapped walker attached?
choices: It calls navController.navigate("booking/{walkerId}")
It sets screen = Screen.CreateBooking(walker) ã the data class carries the walker directly, no string route needed
It stores the walker in a global singleton
It can't ã PawWalk has no way to pass data between screens
steps.explain: Screen.CreateBooking(val walker: Walker) carries its payload as a typed Kotlin property, not a stringly-typed route argument. That's the one real advantage of the hand-rolled approach: no string parsing, the compiler checks it.
steps.nudge: Screen.CreateBooking is a data class with a `walker` property ã what does assigning `screen = Screen.CreateBooking(walker)` do with that property?

checks.hint: Same shape as `CreateBooking`: `data class Reviews(val walker: Walker) : Screen`.

mustNot.hint: This screen needs to carry a `Walker` ã that means `data class`, not `data object` (which carries nothing).

steps.success: That's exactly how you'd extend PawWalk's real navigation model ã one new case, and the compiler forces every `when` over Screen to handle it.

lessons.title: The Walker Screen: State, Actions & a Dialog

md: ## One screen, two very different jobs

`WalkerScreen.kt` is what a signed-in *walker* sees ã completely different from the owner-facing `WalkersScreen` you studied in Lesson 1 (mind the near-identical names!). Where owners browse a list of walkers, a walker sees their own profile plus the bookings assigned to them, each with action buttons: **Accept**, **Decline**, **Start walk**, **Complete**.

Its `WalkerViewModel` holds a small state shape, just like `WalkersViewModel`'s `UiState` from Module 05 ã except this one bundles multiple pieces of data in a single `data class` rather than a sealed interface, because several of the fields (bookings, profile, loading, error) can all be meaningfully non-default at once.

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkerScreen.kt ã the state shape
steps.source:
data class State(
    val bookings: List<Booking> = emptyList(),
    val profile: Walker? = null,
    val loading: Boolean = true,
    val error: String? = null,
)
steps.caption: Every field has a default, so `State()` alone is a valid "nothing loaded yet" state. `profile` and `error` are nullable ã either can simply not exist yet.

md: ## Reacting to a tap: `act()`

Each booking's card shows different buttons depending on `booking.status` ã a `when` maps status strings to a list of `(label, action)` pairs. Tapping one calls `viewModel.act(b.id, action)`, which posts the transition to the backend and reloads:

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkerScreen.kt ã WalkerViewModel.act
steps.caption: `runCatching { ãÎ }` (Module 03) swallows a failed transition rather than crashing the screen ã either way, `load()` re-fetches so the UI reflects whatever the backend now says is true. That's simpler than tracking optimistic UI state by hand, at the cost of one extra network round trip.

steps.q: Why does `act()` call `load()` again after `BookingRepository.transition(...)`, instead of just updating the tapped booking's status locally?
choices: Kotlin requires every ViewModel function to call load()
It re-syncs the whole state from the backend ã the source of truth ã rather than guessing what changed locally, at the cost of one more request
load() is required to dismiss the loading spinner
transition() doesn't actually change anything on the server
steps.explain: Trusting the server's response over a hand-maintained local copy avoids the two ever disagreeing ã simpler code, one extra request.
steps.nudge: What's the tradeoff between trusting the server's fresh answer versus editing local state by hand?

md: ## A dialog is just a composable with an on/off switch

Tap **Edit** on your profile and an `AlertDialog` appears ã Compose's built-in modal. Notice it's driven by the exact `remember { mutableStateOf(false) }` pattern from Module 05: `showEdit` is local UI state, flipped `true` by the Edit tap, and the dialog itself is only placed in the composition `if (showEdit)` is true.

steps.title: ui/screens/WalkerScreen.kt ã showing the dialog
steps.caption: `onDismiss` fires when the user taps outside the dialog or presses Back ã Compose calls it for you. The trailing lambda (the third argument, `onSave`) is the Save button's callback: apply the update, then close.

steps.title: Wire up a details dialog

1. Declare `var showDetails` with `remember { mutableStateOf(false) }`, defaulting to `false`.
2. Below it, add an `if (showDetails)` block that calls `WalkerDetailsDialog(onDismiss = { showDetails = false })`.

checks.hint: `var showDetails by remember { mutableStateOf(false) }` ã the `by` delegate makes `showDetails` read like a plain Boolean.
Guard the dialog with `if (showDetails) { ãÎ }` ã it's only placed in the composition while true.
Inside the block: `WalkerDetailsDialog(onDismiss = { showDetails = false })` ã dismissing flips the flag back off.

lessons.title: Tabs & Scaffolding: How Home Ties It Together

md: ## The tab bar isn't a system component

Unlike iOS's `TabView`, PawWalk's bottom tab bar in `HomeScreen.kt` isn't a built-in Compose or Material 3 component ã it's hand-built from pieces you already know: a `Row`, a `RoundedCornerShape` background, and a `clickable` per tab. That's normal in Compose: when the built-in `NavigationBar` doesn't match a custom design (like PawWalk's pill-shaped, floating HUD bar), you compose your own from primitives.

steps.title: ui/screens/HomeScreen.kt ã HudTabBar
steps.caption: `RoundedCornerShape(50)` on a `Row` this short makes a full pill/capsule shape. Each `Tab` takes a label, whether it's the active one, a click handler, and an `icon` composable ã a parameter of *function type* that itself returns UI, so every tab can show a different icon.

md: ## `Tab`: a private helper with `RowScope` receiver

One new shape in `Tab`'s signature: `private fun RowScope.Tab(...)`. Writing the receiver type (`RowScope`) before the function name makes `Tab` an **extension function** ã it can only be called *inside* a `Row` (or another `RowScope`), and inside its body it gets access to `Row`-only modifiers like `.weight(1f)`, which makes all four tabs share the available width equally.

steps.title: ui/screens/HomeScreen.kt ã Tab
steps.caption: `icon()` calls the passed-in composable right where the icon should render. The active tab gets `c.accent` as its background; every other tab is transparent ã the whole "which tab is selected" visual comes from one Boolean.

steps.q: Why is `Tab` declared as `private fun RowScope.Tab(...)` instead of a plain `private fun Tab(...)`?
choices: RowScope makes the function faster
It's required syntax for any @Composable function
It restricts Tab to being called inside a Row, and unlocks Row-only modifiers like .weight() inside its body
It has no effect ã RowScope is purely documentation
steps.explain: An extension function's receiver type both scopes where it can be called AND grants access to that scope's members ã here, .weight(1f), which is what makes all four tabs share the row equally.
steps.nudge: What does Tab's body use that only makes sense inside a Row ã and what does the RowScope receiver unlock?

md: ## HomeScreen wires the whole flow

Zoom out to `HomeScreen` itself: it's a `Box` (Module 04) layering two things ã a scrollable `Column` of content, and the tab bar pinned to the bottom with `Modifier.align(Alignment.BottomCenter)`. Every tab's `onClick` is a callback parameter (Module 05's "events flow up") that `HomeScreen` doesn't implement itself ã it just forwards taps to whoever constructed it.

That whoever is `MainActivity`, where those callbacks become `screen = Screen.Walkers`-style assignments ã the sealed-interface navigation from Lesson 2. Trace it end to end: tap **Book** in the tab bar ã `HudTabBar`'s `onBook` fires ã `HomeScreen`'s `onBook` parameter fires ã `MainActivity`'s `onBook = { screen = Screen.Walkers }` runs ã `screen` changes ã `Crossfade` swaps to `WalkersScreen`.

steps.title: ui/screens/HomeScreen.kt ã HomeScreen's signature and tab bar placement
steps.caption: Five callback parameters, every one defaulted to a no-op `{}` (or `{ _ in }`-style default) ã Module 05's "opt-in callback" pattern from the iOS course applies here too. `.navigationBarsPadding()` keeps the floating bar clear of the system's gesture bar at the bottom of the screen.

checks.hint: Match the existing tabs' shape: `Tab("Assistant", onClick = onAssistant) { ãÎ }`.
Inside the trailing lambda: `ChatIcon(on.copy(alpha = 0.6f), 15.dp)` ã same dimmed style as the other inactive tabs.

steps.success: That's the whole cost of a new tab: one Tab(...) call. The Row's weight(1f) modifiers mean all five now share the width automatically.

steps.title: Trace a tap across three files
intro: Everything in this module is live in the app. Prove it end to end:
items: Terminal tab 1: `cd apps/backend && uv run fastapi dev` ã leave it running.
In Android Studio, run the app on your Pixel emulator and sign in as a pet owner.
On Home, tap the **Track** tab. Watch `screen` flip to `Screen.Live(...)` in `MainActivity.kt` and `Crossfade` swap in `LiveScreen`.
Tap **Book**. That's `HudTabBar`'s onBook ã `HomeScreen`'s onBook ã `MainActivity`'s `screen = Screen.Walkers` ã the `LazyColumn` of walkers from Lesson 1 appears.

md: ## From tap to walk

Everything you've built so far pays off here. Tap a walker on `WalkersScreen`, and PawWalk opens `CreateBookingScreen` ã a form with a pet picker, a duration choice, a start-time nudge, and a Confirm button that POSTs the whole thing to the backend. This module rebuilds that form and the loop it kicks off.

The screen takes a `Walker` and two callbacks: `onClose` (the back arrow) and `onBooked` (fired once the booking exists on the server). It doesn't know or care *who* opened it ã same "events flow up, state flows down" shape you used for `WalkerScreen` in Module 9.

steps.title: ui/screens/CreateBookingScreen.kt (signature + pet picker)
steps.caption: Five remember { mutableStateOf(...) } fields ã every editable field on the screen has its own slice of state, straight out of Module 5.

md: ### `LaunchedEffect(Unit)` ã a `.task` you already know

You met `LaunchedEffect` in Module 9 for network loads; here it fetches the owner's saved pets *once*, when the screen first appears (`Unit` never changes, so it never restarts). `runCatching { ãÎ }.getOrDefault(emptyList())` is one line doing what a `try`/`catch` would do in three: attempt the call, and if it throws, fall back to an empty list instead of crashing the screen.

That empty-list fallback isn't just error handling ã it's a feature. Look at the form below: `if (pets.isEmpty())` swaps between a free-text field and a row of pet chips. A brand-new owner with no saved pets still types a name by hand; a returning owner taps Mochi. Same form, two experiences, driven by one `if`.

steps.title: ui/screens/CreateBookingScreen.kt (pet picker excerpt)
steps.caption: Trimmed for focus ã the real file adds shape, color, and spacing modifiers to each chip.

steps.q: Why does `CreateBookingScreen` fall back to an empty pet list instead of showing an error when `PetRepository.list()` fails?
choices: Because `runCatching` always returns an empty list no matter what
So the form degrades gracefully to a free-text field instead of blocking the booking entirely
Because pets are optional in the API contract
It doesn't ã a failed fetch crashes the screen
steps.explain: `runCatching { ãÎ }.getOrDefault(emptyList())` turns any thrown exception into an empty list. With no pets loaded, `pets.isEmpty()` is true, and the form shows the plain text field ã booking still works.
steps.nudge: Look at what happens to the screen when `pets` stays `emptyList()` ã is that a dead end or a fallback?

md: ### Duration chips and the `+`/`ã` start time

Duration is a `Row` of three chips ã 30, 45, 60 ã over a hardcoded `DURATIONS` list; tapping one sets `duration = mins`. Same selected/unselected coloring pattern as the pet chips, just a different backing list.

Start time skips a full date-and-time picker on purpose ã the file's doc comment says so directly: *"kept simple ã pick an hour later today ã rather than pulling in a date/time picker dependency for one field."* `hoursFromNow` is just an `Int`, nudged by `+`/`ã` buttons, and `remember(hoursFromNow) { Instant.now().plusSeconds(hoursFromNow * 3600L) }` recomputes the actual `Instant` whenever it changes. That's `remember` with a **key** ã Module 5 showed `remember` with no arguments (compute once, keep forever); passing `hoursFromNow` here means *recompute whenever this key changes*, not on every recomposition.

steps.title: Guard against a minus past zero
prompt: The `ã` button under Start time should stop decrementing once `hoursFromNow` reaches 1 ã nobody can book a walk zero hours from now and land in the past. Add the missing `enabled` condition to the modifier below.
Use `Modifier.clickable(enabled = ..., onClick ...)` ã the click only fires when `enabled` is true.

checks.hint: The condition is `enabled = hoursFromNow > 1` ã only allow the decrement while there's room above 1.
Keep the trailing lambda `{ hoursFromNow-- }` as the click action.

steps.success: That's the exact guard from CreateBookingScreen.kt. A disabled clickable is the Compose way to grey out a control ã no separate 'enabled' visual state to manage by hand.

md: ## What Confirm booking actually does

The Confirm button calls a local `submit()` function, not the view model directly. `submit()` has one job before it ever touches the network: **validate client-side first**, so a bad input never becomes a wasted round trip.

steps.title: ui/screens/CreateBookingScreen.kt (submit)
steps.caption: One check today: a blank dog name. dogName.trim() strips whitespace before it's sent ã same discipline as the iOS course's trimmingCharacters.

md: ### `dogName.isBlank()` vs `dogName.isEmpty()`

Kotlin's `String` has both. `isEmpty()` is true only for `""` ã zero characters. `isBlank()` is true for `""` **and** for a string made entirely of whitespace, like `"   "`. A dog "named" three spaces would pass `isEmpty()` but correctly fails `isBlank()` ã that's why the form checks `isBlank()`, exactly like the iOS form trims before checking `isEmpty` for the same reason, just one Kotlin function instead of two Swift steps.
If validation fails, `submit()` returns immediately ã no network call, no view model touched. Only a validated request reaches `viewModel.book(...)`, which hands off to the repository:

steps.title: ui/screens/CreateBookingViewModel.kt
steps.caption: The whole file. Four UiState cases, one function ã the same StateFlow shape every ViewModel in this course has used since Module 5.

md: ### Cents math the server owns ã not the client

Notice what's missing from `CreateBookingRequest`: a price. The client sends `walkerId`, `dogName`, `startTime`, `durationMinutes` ã never a total. `BookingRepository.createBooking` just forwards the request to Retrofit; the *server* multiplies the walker's `pricePer30MinCents` by the chosen duration and writes `priceCents` onto the `Booking` it hands back. The client only ever *displays* cents math (`priceCents / 100.0` ã Module 1), it never *computes* the number that determines what gets charged. Never trust the device the user controls with the money math.

When the request succeeds, `state` becomes `UiState.Success(booking)` ã and back in the screen, a `LaunchedEffect(state)` watches for exactly that case and calls `onBooked()`, closing the loop back to whoever presented this screen.

steps.q: Where does the final `priceCents` on a new booking get computed?
choices: In `CreateBookingScreen`, from `duration` and the walker's price
In `CreateBookingViewModel.book`, before sending the request
On the backend server, from the walker's rate and the requested duration
It's hardcoded to the walker's 30-minute price regardless of duration
steps.explain: `CreateBookingRequest` never carries a price field ã only walkerId, dogName, startTime, and durationMinutes. The server computes priceCents and returns it on the Booking. The client that requests a walk is never the client trusted to price it.
steps.nudge: Look at what fields `CreateBookingRequest` actually has ã is a price one of them?

steps.title: Rebuild the happy/sad split
prompt: `book()` lost its try/catch. Rebuild the body of the `viewModelScope.launch` block: set `_state.value` to `UiState.Loading`, then assign the result of a `try`/`catch` to `_state.value` ã on success wrap the booking from `BookingRepository.createBooking(...)` in `UiState.Success`, on failure wrap `e.message ?: "Couldn't create booking"` in `UiState.Error`.

checks.hint: First line: flip the state to `_state.value = UiState.Loading` before the network call starts.
Assign the whole try/catch expression to `_state.value` ã a `try` block in Kotlin evaluates to a value.
On success, wrap the created booking: `UiState.Success(booking)`.
On failure: `catch (e: Exception) { UiState.Error(e.message ?: "Couldn't create booking") }`.

steps.success: That's the real book() function. Loading, then a try/catch that resolves to exactly one of two states ã the same shape you'll see in every ViewModel that talks to the network.

md: ## A list, a status pill, and a bug that got fixed here

`BookingsScreen` is Module 9's `LazyColumn` pattern again: a `when` over a `UiState` (Loading / Success / Error), and inside Success a `LazyColumn` keyed by `booking.id`. Nothing new in the list mechanics ã the new part is what each row shows and does.

steps.title: ui/screens/BookingsViewModel.kt
steps.caption: init { load() } ã the view model fetches the moment it's created, so the screen never shows a blank frame before the first load kicks off.

md: ### `cancel()` re-fetches instead of patching one row

Compare `cancel()` to what you might expect: it doesn't find the cancelled booking in the list and flip its status locally. It calls `BookingRepository.cancelBooking(bookingId)` on the server, then just calls `load()` again ã the whole list, fresh from the source of truth. With a handful of bookings, re-fetching is simpler and can't drift out of sync with the server. No index bookkeeping, no partial updates to get wrong.
### The bug: what counts as *cancellable*, and what counts as *upcoming*
Each row only shows a Cancel button for bookings the owner can actually still back out of:

steps.title: ui/screens/BookingsScreen.kt (BookingCard excerpt)
steps.caption: Cancellable: pending or confirmed only. Once a walk is in_progress, completed, or already cancelled, the button never renders ã same idea as the iOS canCancel.

md: That `cancellable` check is local to the bookings list. A related ã and easy to get wrong ã question lives elsewhere: **which bookings count as "upcoming"?** Home's "Next walk" card and the live-tracking screen both need to pick *one* booking out of the list to feature. The first version of this code picked any booking whose status wasn't `cancelled` ã which meant a booking that had already run to `completed` could still get picked as the "next" walk, or auto-selected for live tracking, because `completed != cancelled` too.

The fix: add a shared `isActive` property to the `Booking` model itself, with an explicit allowlist instead of a denylist ã `pending`, `confirmed`, or `in_progress` count as active; everything else (`completed`, `cancelled`) doesn't. One property, defined once, used everywhere a screen needs to ask "is this walk still coming up or happening now?"

steps.title: data/Models.kt (Booking, excerpt)
steps.caption: A computed property, not a stored field ã isActive is recalculated from status every time it's read, so it can never go stale.

steps.q: Why did the old `status != "cancelled"` check pick the wrong booking for Home's "Next walk" card?
choices: It threw an exception on completed bookings
`completed` also satisfies `status != "cancelled"`, so a walk that already finished could still be treated as upcoming
It only checked the first booking in the list, ignoring the rest
`cancelled` bookings were being shown as upcoming instead
steps.explain: A denylist ("not cancelled") is true for completed, in_progress, confirmed, and pending alike ã completed walks slipped through. The fix switched to an allowlist: isActive is true only for pending, confirmed, or in_progress.
steps.nudge: `status != "cancelled"` is true for every status except one. Which other status shouldn't count as 'upcoming', but still passes that check?

steps.title: Write isActive
prompt: Add the `isActive` computed property to `Booking`, matching the real one in `Models.kt`: `true` when `status` is `"pending"`, `"confirmed"`, or `"in_progress"` ã `false` for anything else (`completed`, `cancelled`).

checks.hint: Declare it as `val isActive: Boolean get() = ãÎ` ã a computed property, not a function.
First allowed status: `status == "pending"`.
Second allowed status, joined with `||`: `status == "confirmed"`.
Third allowed status: `status == "in_progress"` ã the underscore matters, it must match the backend's exact string.

mustNot.hint: That's the old, buggy denylist ã it lets `completed` through too. Write the allowlist instead: three `==` checks joined by `||`.

steps.success: You just rebuilt the real bug fix ã an allowlist beats a denylist whenever 'not one bad value' isn't the same as 'one specific good value'.

md: ## Status pills and the shape of the payment flow
One more thing worth reading before this module ends: how a booking's `status` string becomes color on screen, and where money actually changes hands.

steps.title: ui/screens/BookingsScreen.kt (StatusPill)
steps.caption: when's else branch covers both pending and confirmed with one accent color ã only three statuses earn a distinct color.

md: ### `status.replace('_', ' ')`

One line of real string polish: the raw status from the server is `"in_progress"` ã a fine identifier, an ugly label. `.replace('_', ' ')` swaps every underscore for a space before the text ever reaches the screen, so the pill reads *in progress* instead of *in_progress*. Small, but it's the difference between code-shaped text and human-shaped text.
### Where does the money actually move?

Booking a walk creates it as `pending` ã no charge yet. According to `docs/API-CONTRACT.md`, paying happens through a separate endpoint: `POST /payments/intent`, which returns a Stripe `client_secret` (or an offline stub if Stripe isn't configured on the backend ã same response shape either way, so the client code never needs to know which mode it's in). On a real device, that `client_secret` is where Stripe's **PaymentSheet** would slot in: hand it the secret, PaymentSheet renders Stripe's own card-entry UI, and a webhook on the backend (`POST /payments/webhook`) flips the booking to `confirmed` once Stripe confirms the charge succeeded.

That means PawWalk's booking flow and its payment flow are two separate server calls, on purpose ã you can have a `pending` booking sitting unpaid, and the payment step is what would eventually push it forward. This course builds the booking half in full; the PaymentSheet integration itself is a stretch goal for later, once the rest of the app is second nature.

steps.q: In the real API contract, what confirms a booking after payment succeeds?
choices: The Android app sets status to confirmed itself, right after PaymentSheet closes
A Stripe webhook hits the backend (`POST /payments/webhook`), and the server flips the booking to confirmed
The walker manually confirms it in their app
Bookings are confirmed at creation time, before any payment
steps.explain: The client never marks its own payment as successful ã that would mean trusting the device again, the same mistake price math would be. Stripe calls the backend's webhook directly; only then does the server flip the booking's status.
steps.nudge: Think back to the isActive lesson: who's allowed to be the source of truth for anything money-related ã the phone, or the server?

steps.title: Format a status for display
prompt: Write a one-line function `displayStatus` that takes a raw booking `status` string and returns it with underscores replaced by spaces ã matching what `StatusPill` does to its label before showing it.

checks.hint: Return `status.replace('_', ' ')` ã single-quoted chars for both the underscore and the space, same as StatusPill.

mustNot.hint: Use single-quote `Char` literals (`'_'`, `' '`), not double-quoted strings ã that's what the real StatusPill code uses.

steps.success: "in_progress" becomes "in progress" ã one call, real polish. Small readability wins like this are worth the one extra line.

md: ## Watching a walk happen

Every screen so far has been *ask once, show once*: load the walkers, load a booking, done. Live tracking is different ã while a walk is in progress, the walker's phone keeps producing new GPS points, and the owner's phone should show each dot **the moment it lands**, not a beat later.

`ui/screens/LiveScreen.kt` is that screen: a full-bleed `Canvas` drawing the route the walker has taken, with a HUD of stats (Elapsed, Distance, Pace) floating on top. It's the same product you saw on the iOS track ã same idea, same backend, a Kotlin file instead of a Swift one.

This module takes `LiveScreen.kt` and its `LiveViewModel.kt` apart piece by piece: how fixes arrive over the network, how a location is *published* from the walker's phone, how the route gets drawn without an actual map, and how Android asks permission to use the GPS at all.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveViewModel.kt (the shell)
steps.caption: The same private-MutableStateFlow / public-StateFlow shape every view model in this course has used since Module 6. A Fix is one GPS point; Phase names every state the screen can be in ã no booking, waiting on GPS, streaming, permission denied, or the socket died.

md: ## Reading the screen's state

`LiveScreen` is a thin consumer of that `State`: it collects it, and switches its HUD text on `phase`. No network code, no location code ã all of that lives in the view model, exactly the split you learned in Module 6 (state lives in the view model; composables only *read* it).
Two small helpers turn raw numbers into HUD text ã `elapsedLabel` formats seconds as `07:42`, and `distanceLabel` picks metres or kilometres depending on how far the walk has gone.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveScreen.kt ã distanceLabel
steps.caption: Under 1000 metres, show whole metres. Past that, switch to kilometres with two decimal places via Kotlin's `String.format`-style `.format(...)` extension ã the same C-style `%.2f` you'd use in Swift's `String(format:)`.

steps.q: `distanceLabel` calls `LiveViewModel.distanceMeters(fixes)` ã a function attached to the *companion object*, not an instance. Why put it there instead of as a normal method on the view model?
choices: Companion object functions run faster than instance methods

It's pure math over a `List<Fix>` with no dependency on the view model's own state ã a `companion object` function is Kotlin's way to expose something call-able without an instance, like Swift's `static func`
Kotlin requires all math functions to live in a companion object
It's a typo ã it should be a regular method
steps.explain: distanceMeters only needs the list of fixes you hand it ã it doesn't read or write anything on `this`. Making it a companion-object function documents that: you can call `LiveViewModel.distanceMeters(someList)` from a preview or a test with no view model instance at all.
steps.nudge: Does `distanceMeters` read `_state` or any other property of the view model? What does that tell you about whether it needs an instance?

steps.title: Format the elapsed time
prompt: Rebuild `elapsedLabel` from `LiveScreen.kt`. Given `startedAtMs` (nullable) and `nowMs`:

1. If `startedAtMs` is `null`, return `"00:00"` immediately.
2. Otherwise compute whole seconds elapsed: `(nowMs - startedAtMs) / 1000`, and clamp it to never go negative with `.coerceAtLeast(0)`.
3. Return it formatted as minutes:seconds, each zero-padded to 2 digits, using `"%02d:%02d".format(s / 60, s % 60)`.

checks.hint: Guard the null case first: `if (startedAtMs == null) return "00:00"`.
Clamp the elapsed seconds with `.coerceAtLeast(0)` ã clock skew could otherwise produce a negative value.
Format with `"%02d:%02d".format(s / 60, s % 60)` ã minutes first, then seconds, both zero-padded.

mustNot.hint: Kotlin strings have their own `.format(...)` extension ã you don't need Java's `String.format(...)` here.

steps.success: That's the exact clock the HUD's Elapsed readout runs on, ticking once a second.

lessons.title: Sockets & Location: Fixes In, Fixes Out

md: ## One socket per booking

`connect(bookingId)` runs in `viewModelScope.launch` (Module 3's coroutine-launching pattern). It resolves *which* booking to track ã the one it's given, or the caller's most recent active booking ã then reads the bearer token from `TokenStore` (Module 8) and opens a WebSocket.

A WebSocket is a connection that, once opened, stays open: either side can send a message at any time, instead of the request-reply-done shape every other screen in this app uses. PawWalk opens one WebSocket per booking ã the walker's phone *sends* GPS fixes up the wire, and every phone watching that booking *receives* them.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveViewModel.kt ã openSocket
steps.caption: http/https becomes ws/wss the same way on Android as anywhere else ã WebSockets have their own URL schemes, and swapping the prefix preserves whether the connection is encrypted. The token rides along as a query param because a raw WebSocket handshake from a native client can't set an Authorization header.

steps.q: Why does the URL carry the token as `?token=$token` instead of an `Authorization: Bearer ãÎ` header, the way every other PawWalk request does (Module 8)?
choices: It's less secure but faster to type
WebSockets don't support headers at all
The initial WebSocket handshake from a native client can't set a custom header, so the token has to ride in the URL as a query parameter instead
The backend only accepts tokens in query params, never headers
steps.explain: Every other endpoint in this app attaches the token via the OkHttp interceptor from Module 8, which sets a real header. A WebSocket's opening handshake doesn't give native clients that option, so the query string is the accepted workaround ã the backend reads `?token=` specifically for this one endpoint.
steps.nudge: Re-read the API contract's note about `?token=` ã what kind of client is it calling out as unable to set a header here?

steps.title: Build the WebSocket URL
prompt: `wsBase` is already rewritten to `ws`/`wss` above the marker. Finish `openSocket` by building the `url` line, where the marker is:
Build the `url` string by gluing together, in order: `wsBase`, then `/ws/track/`, then `bookingId`, then `?token=`, then `token` ã all via `$name` string templates in one double-quoted string.

checks.hint: Build the url with `$name` templates: `"$wsBase/ws/track/$bookingId?token=$token"` ã no braces needed, these are simple names.

mustNot.hint: Use a single string template, not `+` concatenation, to glue the pieces together.

steps.success: That's the exact url line shipping in LiveViewModel.kt ã one socket per booking, token attached.

md: ## The other direction: publishing a fix

Lesson so far was fixes flowing *in*. On the walker's phone, fixes also flow *out*: Android's location APIs hand `LiveViewModel` a new `Location` object, and `publish(lat, lng)` sends it up the same socket as a tiny hand-written JSON string.

For a three-field message, building JSON by hand with a Kotlin **triple-quoted string** (`"""ãÎ"""`) beats a whole `@Serializable` data class ã the same call the iOS course made with `JSONSerialization` for one-off payloads. Triple-quoted strings don't need escaped inner quotes, which is why the JSON's own `"` characters read cleanly.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveViewModel.kt ã publish
steps.caption: socket?.send(...) ã the safe call means nothing happens if the socket hasn't connected yet, no crash, no if-check needed. lat and lng interpolate straight into the triple-quoted JSON with $name, no braces required.

steps.q: `socket?.send(...)` uses the safe-call operator `?.` instead of a plain `.`. What does that buy `publish`?
choices: It makes the send happen asynchronously
If `socket` is null (not connected yet, or already closed), the whole expression evaluates to null and nothing happens ã no crash, no explicit if-check needed
It retries the send automatically if it fails
It converts the socket to a nullable type
steps.explain: `?.` is Kotlin's nullability operator from Module 2: call the method only if the receiver isn't null, otherwise short-circuit to null. Here that means a location update that arrives before the socket is open (or after it's been torn down) is silently dropped instead of throwing a NullPointerException.
steps.nudge: Compare `socket?.send(...)` to `socket!!.send(...)` ã what would the second one do if `socket` were null?

md: ## No map, no map tiles

There's no Google Maps tile here ã real map tiles cost money and need an API key. `LiveScreen`'s route is *drawn*, with Compose's low-level **`Canvas`** composable: it hands you a drawing scope with methods like `drawLine`, `drawPath`, and `drawCircle`, and you paint directly, pixel by pixel. Think whiteboard, not map widget.

To turn a list of `(lat, lng)` fixes into pixel coordinates, `LiveScreen` projects them: longitude gets squished by `cos(midLat)` ã degrees of longitude shrink the farther you get from the equator, so skipping this step stretches every route sideways ã then the whole set is scaled and centered to fit a padded rectangle on screen. Relative shape survives; that's all a route sketch needs.

> Where Maps Compose would replace this: if PawWalk added a Google Maps API key, `MapCanvas`'s hand-rolled projection and `drawPath` call would become a `Polyline` composable from the `maps-compose` library, drawn over real map tiles instead of a blank canvas. Same fixes, same `Fix` list ã just a fancier renderer.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveScreen.kt ã projecting fixes to screen space
steps.source:
val midLat = (fixes.minOf { it.lat } + fixes.maxOf { it.lat }) / 2
val cosLat = cos(Math.toRadians(midLat))
fun projX(f: LiveViewModel.Fix) = f.lng * cosLat
fun projY(f: LiveViewModel.Fix) = f.lat
val minX = fixes.minOf { projX(it) }; val maxX = fixes.maxOf { projX(it) }
val minY = fixes.minOf { projY(it) }; val maxY = fixes.maxOf { projY(it) }
val spanX = max(maxX - minX, 1e-12); val spanY = max(maxY - minY, 1e-12)
val rect = Rect(60f, 200f, size.width - 60f, size.height - 220f)
val sc = min(rect.width / spanX, rect.height / spanY)
steps.caption: minOf/maxOf with a trailing lambda (Module 2) find the bounding box of every fix. spanX/spanY use max(..., 1e-12) so a single fix (zero span) never divides by zero. sc is the one scale factor ã shared by X and Y ã that fits the whole route inside rect without distorting it.

steps.q: Why does `sc` take `min(rect.width / spanX, rect.height / spanY)` ã the *smaller* of the two ratios ã instead of scaling X and Y independently?
choices: The smaller ratio is always more accurate for GPS math
Using one shared scale factor (the tighter-fitting one) keeps the route's proportions correct ã scaling X and Y independently would stretch or squash the shape to fill the rectangle
It's a performance optimization to avoid extra divisions
Compose requires width and height to scale identically
steps.explain: A route that goes twice as far north as it does east should look that way on screen. Picking one scale (the smaller ratio, so the bigger dimension still fits) preserves that proportion; two independent scales would distort every shape into whatever aspect ratio the rectangle happens to have.
steps.nudge: If a walk went in a perfect straight line east-to-west and you scaled X and Y with two different numbers, would it still look straight?

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveScreen.kt ã the pulsing ping
steps.source:
val cp = pts.last()
drawCircle(c.accent.copy(alpha = 0.45f * (1f - ping)), 12f + 22f * ping, cp)
drawCircle(c.accent, 8f, cp)
drawCircle(on, 8f, cp, style = Stroke(2.5f))
steps.caption: ping is an animated Float from 0 to 1, driven by rememberInfiniteTransition (Compose's built-in repeating-animation tool) back up in LiveScreen ã every frame it advances, Canvas redraws, and this block runs again with a new value. As ping grows, the outer ring's radius grows (12f + 22f * ping) while its alpha fades toward zero: a ring that expands and disappears, forever, with zero manual @State bookkeeping.

steps.title: Draw the route line
prompt: Given a `List<Offset>` named `pts` (already projected to screen coordinates) and `c.accent` (the theme's accent `Color`), draw the route as a stroked path when there's more than one point:

1. Guard: only proceed `if (pts.size > 1)`.
2. Build a `Path()` with `.apply { ... }`: `moveTo(pts[0].x, pts[0].y)`, then for every remaining point call `lineTo(it.x, it.y)` via `pts.drop(1).forEach { ... }`.
3. Draw it with `drawPath(route, c.accent, style = Stroke(width = 3.5f, cap = StrokeCap.Round, join = StrokeJoin.Round))`.
steps.starter:
// pts: List<Offset>, c.accent: Color
// your code here

checks.hint: Guard with `if (pts.size > 1)` ã a path needs at least two points to draw a line.
Start the path at the first point: `moveTo(pts[0].x, pts[0].y)`.
For the rest of the points, `pts.drop(1).forEach { lineTo(it.x, it.y) }` ã `drop(1)` skips the point you already moveTo'd.

mustNot.hint: Don't forEach over ALL of `pts` for lineTo ã the first point should be a moveTo, only the rest are lineTo.

steps.success: That's the exact path-building block drawing every route on the Live screen.

md: ## Android gatekeeps location too

Just like iOS, Android won't hand an app GPS access silently ã the user has to grant a **runtime permission**, and the app has to ask. `LiveScreen` asks with `rememberLauncherForActivityResult`, a Compose tool that wraps Android's permission-request flow into something you call like a function and get a callback from.

PawWalk asks for two related permissions at once ã `ACCESS_FINE_LOCATION` (precise GPS) and `ACCESS_COARSE_LOCATION` (approximate, network-based) ã because some devices grant only the coarse one. The `grants` map in the callback tells you which ones the user actually said yes to.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveScreen.kt ã requesting permission
steps.caption: grants.values.any { it } ã a trailing lambda over the map's values (Module 2's `it` shorthand): true if AT LEAST ONE of the two permissions was granted. LaunchedEffect(Unit) (Module 5) runs this exactly once when the screen first appears ã open the socket and ask permission together.

steps.q: The callback checks `grants.values.any { it }` rather than requiring *both* permissions to be granted. Why accept just one?
choices: It's a bug ã both permissions should be required

Fine and coarse location are separate permissions, but either one is enough to get a location fix ã some devices or user choices grant only the coarse one, so requiring both would needlessly lock those users out
Android always grants both permissions together, so the check is redundant
any { it } actually means "all", not "at least one"
steps.explain: Fine and coarse are independent permissions the user can grant separately. Since the goal is just "can we get SOME location", accepting either one is the more permissive ã and correct ã check. Requiring both would break tracking for users who only allowed coarse location.
steps.nudge: What does `.any { it }` return if only one of the two values in `grants` is true?

md: ## FusedLocationProvider: the actual GPS tap

Once permission is granted, `startLocationUpdates()` opens the real tap: Google Play services' **`FusedLocationProviderClient`**, which blends GPS, Wi-Fi, and cell data into one best-effort location stream ã smarter than talking to raw GPS hardware directly, and it's what almost every Android app uses.

A `LocationRequest` configures the stream: `Priority.PRIORITY_HIGH_ACCURACY` asks for the best the device can do (mirroring iOS's `kCLLocationAccuracyBest`), a interval in milliseconds sets how often to check, and `setMinUpdateDistanceMeters(5f)` ã like iOS's `distanceFilter` ã means a fix only arrives after the device has moved at least 5 metres, filtering out GPS jitter from a phone sitting still.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveViewModel.kt ã startLocationUpdates
steps.caption: @SuppressLint("MissingPermission") tells Android Studio's linter "yes, I know this needs a permission ã I checked it before calling this function" (LiveScreen only calls startLocationUpdates() after grants succeed). The try/catch is a safety net for the rare case Android still refuses at the system level.

md: ## The callback: fixes arriving from the OS

`FusedLocationProviderClient` delivers updates through a `LocationCallback` ã an object with an `onLocationResult` method the OS calls whenever a new batch of locations is ready. This is Android's version of the delegate pattern: instead of a closure you pass inline, you subclass (or, as here, build an anonymous `object :`) a callback type and override its method.
`result.lastLocation` is nullable ã a batch could theoretically arrive empty ã so it's read with `?.let { ... }`, Kotlin's "if this isn't null, run this block" idiom from Module 2.

steps.title: ui/screens/LiveViewModel.kt ã locationCallback
steps.caption: object : LocationCallback() { ... } builds a one-off anonymous subclass ã no separate named class needed for a single override. Every new fix flows straight into publish(lat, lng), the function from lesson 2 that sends it up the socket.

steps.title: Handle a denied permission
prompt: Write `onPermissionDenied()` ã the function `LiveScreen` calls when the user rejects both location permissions.
It should update `_state` so that `phase` becomes `Phase.DENIED`, keeping every other field on the state the same. Use `_state.update { it.copy(phase = Phase.DENIED) }`.

checks.hint: Update the state with `_state.update { it.copy(phase = Phase.DENIED) }` ã `.copy` keeps every other field, only `phase` changes.

mustNot.hint: Use `.update { it.copy(...) }`, not direct assignment to `.value` ã `.update` is the safe way to transform a MutableStateFlow.

steps.success: That's the exact one-liner in LiveViewModel.kt ã and it's what flips the HUD's status pill to "Location access is off."

steps.q: The owner and the walker are both watching the same booking's Live screen. Whose phone actually calls `startLocationUpdates()` and `publish(lat, lng)` in a way that matters?
choices: Only the owner's ã the owner tracks the walker
Only the walker's ã the walker's phone is the one producing real GPS fixes to publish; the owner's phone opens the same socket but only ever receives
Both phones publish fixes and the backend averages them
Neither ã GPS fixes come from the backend's own server-side location service
steps.explain: Both phones run the identical LiveScreen/LiveViewModel code and both request location permission, but only the walker's fixes are meaningful ã theirs is the phone actually moving with the dog. The owner's device technically starts location updates too (same code path), but what matters for the HUD is the position stream arriving over the socket, which only reflects the walker's real movement.
steps.nudge: Think about which phone is physically on the walk. Does the code care which role the current user has, or does it just run the same logic either way?

md: ## The last screen

PawWalk has one screen you haven't opened yet: the **Assistant** ã a little chat where an owner types *"a walker in the Mission for my husky tomorrow at 3pm"* and gets back suggested walkers they can book with one tap.

Here's the good news for your final module: there is **nothing new** in it. The AI lives on the backend (`POST /assistant/chat` parses the sentence and picks walkers). The app's job is the same loop you've built eleven times: send a request, decode JSON, keep state in a `ViewModel`, render it in Compose.
So this module is a victory lap. You'll read the two real files behind the screen ã `AssistantScreen.kt` and `AssistantViewModel.kt` ã and recognize every single line.

steps.title: AssistantScreen.kt (top of the composable)
steps.caption: Four familiar friends: `collectAsState()` for the two StateFlows (Module 5), `remember { mutableStateOf("") }` for the local text field draft (also Module 5), and a `LaunchedEffect` that reacts to `messages.size` changing.

steps.q: `viewModel: AssistantViewModel = viewModel()` ã what does giving the parameter a default value of `viewModel()` buy you?
choices: It's required syntax with no real effect
The caller can pass a specific view model for previews/tests, but normally just omits the argument and gets one scoped to this screen automatically
It forces the view model to be recreated on every recomposition
It makes the view model shared across every screen in the app
steps.explain: A default parameter is a normal Kotlin feature (Module 1) put to a very Android-specific use: most call sites write `AssistantScreen(onClose = ..., onBook = ...)` and never mention the view model at all ã Compose's `viewModel()` helper looks one up (or creates it) scoped to the screen. Tests or previews can still override it by passing their own.
steps.nudge: Think about what a *default argument* means for the 99% of call sites that don't pass it explicitly.

steps.title: AssistantScreen.kt (list + typing indicator)
steps.caption: The transcript is a `LazyColumn` (Module 9) of `MessageBubble`s. The spinner is just one more `item {}` that appears only while `sending` is true ã no separate overlay, no special-casing.

md: ## Auto-scroll, the Compose way

That `LaunchedEffect(messages.size)` block at the top is the whole auto-scroll feature. `LaunchedEffect` runs its block whenever its key ã here, `messages.size` ã changes. Append one message, the size changes by one, the effect re-runs, `listState.animateScrollToItem(messages.size - 1)` glides the list down to the newest bubble.
That's it. No delegate methods, no manual scroll math ã a state change triggers a side effect, the same pattern you've relied on since state entered the course.

steps.q: Why key the `LaunchedEffect` on `messages.size` instead of on `messages` itself (the whole list)?
choices: `size` is required ã Compose can't key on a List
Either would work here since a new message always changes the size too, but keying on the smaller value is simpler and just as correct for this screen
Keying on `messages` would scroll to the top instead of the bottom
`size` makes the effect run on every recomposition, not just when a message is added
steps.explain: Every new message increases the count by exactly one, so watching `size` catches every append ã and an `Int` is about as simple a key as you can hand `LaunchedEffect`. You could key on `messages` too; `size` is just the leaner choice here.
steps.nudge: What actually changes every time a message is appended ã and is there a simpler value than the whole list to watch for that?

steps.title: Wire the send button
prompt: The send button's `clickable` action, three lines, in order:

1. Copy `draft` into a `val` named `text`.
2. Clear `draft` by assigning it an empty string ã the field empties instantly.
3. Call `viewModel.send(text)`, passing the **copy**.
Why copy first? By the time `send` finishes reading its argument, you want `draft` already empty on screen ã copy freezes the message before the field is wiped.

checks.hint: First: save `draft` into a `val` named `text` ã you're about to erase the field.
Second: assign the empty string to `draft` so the text field clears right away.
Third: call `viewModel.send(text)`, passing the frozen copy, not `draft`.

mustNot.hint: You just cleared `draft` ã pass the copy `text` instead, or the assistant gets an empty string.

steps.success: That's the real click handler from AssistantScreen.kt ã and the last Kotlin you'll type in this course.

steps.title: data/Models.kt (assistant section)
steps.caption: The same `@SerialName` dance you've done since Module 4: snake_case JSON in, camelCase Kotlin out. `draftBooking` is nullable ã the backend only fills it in when it's confident enough to pre-fill a booking.

steps.q: `suggestedWalkers` is `List<String>`, not `List<Walker>`. What are those strings?
choices: Walker names, ready to show on screen
Walker IDs ã the app looks each one up in the walkers it already fetched
URLs of walker profile photos
Raw JSON blobs the app has to decode again
steps.explain: The backend sends just IDs and keeps the payload tiny. The view model already fetched every walker once and stashed them in a map keyed by ID ã so turning an ID into a full record (name, rating, price) is one map lookup.
steps.nudge: Think about what's cheapest for the backend to send, given the app already knows how to fetch walkers.

steps.title: ui/screens/AssistantViewModel.kt
steps.caption: Under forty lines, and you know every trick in them: `MutableStateFlow` + `StateFlow` (Module 5), `viewModelScope.launch` + `try`/`catch`/`finally` (Modules 3 & 8), a transcript that's just a `data class` in a `List`.

md: ## Reading it like a senior dev

**`Message` is nested inside the view model** ã it only exists to describe this transcript, so it lives with it. Nesting a `data class` inside a class you already know how to write (Module 2) costs nothing new.

**`_messages` and `_sending` are private `MutableStateFlow`s; `messages` and `sending` are the public, read-only `StateFlow` views.** The screen can *collect* them but only the view model can *change* them ã the same one-way-traffic rule from `WalkersViewModel` in Module 5.

**`walkersById` is fetched once and cached.** The `if (walkersById.isEmpty())` guard means the second and third messages in a conversation don't re-fetch the whole walker list ã only the very first `send()` pays that cost.

**`send` appends the user's bubble *before* the network call.** The chat feels instant even on slow Wi-Fi. Then `_sending.value = true` shows the spinner, and `finally { _sending.value = false }` guarantees it turns off ã success or failure, the spinner always stops. On failure, `catch` appends an apology bubble instead of crashing the screen.

steps.q: Why does `_sending.value = false` live inside a `finally` block instead of right after the `try` block?
choices: `finally` is required syntax in Kotlin whenever there's a `catch`
`finally` runs no matter which way the block exits ã a clean reply or a caught exception ã so the spinner always turns off either way
It makes the coroutine run faster
Code after the `try` block never runs if there's a `catch`
steps.explain: If you put `_sending.value = false` only after the `try`, a thrown exception would jump straight to `catch` and skip it ã leaving the spinner stuck on. `finally` runs on every exit path, success or failure, so you never have to remember to reset the flag twice.
steps.nudge: What's the one thing `finally` guarantees that placing code after `try` doesn't?

steps.title: Resolve IDs to walkers
prompt: Time to type the cleverest line in the file. After decoding `reply`, add **one line** that turns `reply.suggestedWalkers` (a list of walker ID strings) into full `Walker` records using the `walkersById` map. Store the result in a `val` named `walkers` ã the line below expects that exact name.

Use `mapNotNull`, because a map lookup with `[]` returns something that might be missing: an ID the app doesn't recognize (say, a walker added a second ago) comes back as nothing, and `mapNotNull` quietly drops those instead of crashing.

checks.hint: Store the result in `val walkers = reply.suggestedWalkers.mapNotNull { ... }` ã the append line below uses that exact name.
Inside the lambda, look each ID up with `walkersById[it]` ã `it` is the implicit name for the single lambda parameter (Module 3).

md: ## A guided tour, no new vocabulary

Two screens are left in PawWalk that this course never stopped to name: **PetsScreen** and **ProfileScreen**. You're not going to learn anything new from them ã that's the point of this lesson. Read them and notice you can already explain every line.

steps.title: ui/screens/PetsScreen.kt (view model)
steps.caption: A `sealed interface UiState` with Loading/Success/Error (Module 3), a `StateFlow` (Module 5), a `suspend`-backed repository call in `viewModelScope.launch` (Module 4). You could have written this file yourself back in Module 8.

steps.q: What does `runCatching { PetRepository.create(request) }` buy over a plain `try`/`catch` in `add`?
choices: It's the only way to call a suspend function
It wraps the call's success-or-failure into a single value that `add` deliberately ignores here ã either way, `load()` still runs to refresh the list
It retries the request automatically on failure
It makes the request run on a background thread
steps.explain: `runCatching` is a stdlib helper that turns a thrown exception into a returned value instead of propagating it. `add` doesn't need to branch on the outcome ã whether the create succeeded or failed, it wants to call `load()` next to refresh the pets list from the server, and `runCatching` makes that one line instead of a `try`/`catch` block.
steps.nudge: Look at what runs on the line right after ã does `add` care about the specific result of the create call, or just that it's done trying?

steps.title: ui/screens/ProfileScreen.kt (top of the composable)
steps.caption: `ProfileScreen` doesn't even have its own ViewModel ã the screen fetches `pets` itself in a `LaunchedEffect(Unit)`, which runs its block exactly once when the screen first appears (the key `Unit` never changes, so the effect never re-runs). Callbacks (`onClose`, `onBookings`, `onPets`, `onLogout`) are passed in rather than owned here ã events flow up, same as always.

steps.q: Why does `ProfileScreen` take five callback parameters (`onClose`, `onBookings`, `onPets`, `onLogout`, plus `user`) instead of navigating directly with a `NavController`?
choices: Compose doesn't allow composables to trigger navigation
It keeps the composable decoupled from *how* navigation works ã the caller (wired up near the NavHost) decides what each action actually does
It's slower to call NavController directly, so callbacks are a performance optimization
Callbacks are required for any function with more than two parameters
steps.explain: This is state-hoisting's sibling principle for events: a reusable composable shouldn't reach out and grab a `NavController` itself ã it just reports "the user tapped bookings" via `onBookings()` and lets whoever composes it decide what that means (usually `navController.navigate(...)`, wired up once near the `NavHost`). Same idea as Module 9's navigation graph, just applied to a leaf screen.
steps.nudge: Where in the app does something actually call `navController.navigate(...)` for this screen ã inside ProfileScreen, or somewhere closer to the NavHost?

steps.solution: val pets = runCatching { PetRepository.list() }.getOrDefault(emptyList())

checks.hint: Wrap `PetRepository.list()` in `runCatching { ... }`, storing the result in `val pets`.
Chain `.getOrDefault(emptyList())` after the `runCatching` block to fall back to an empty list on failure.

steps.success: That's the exact line from ProfileScreen.kt. runCatching + getOrDefault is a one-line try/catch for whenever you just want a safe fallback value.

md: ## Look how far you've come
Twelve modules ago, `val` versus `var` was news. Now look at what you can read ã and write:

- **Kotlin itself** ã constants, nullability, data classes, lambdas, `when`, sealed interfaces (Modules 1ã3)
- **Serializable models & coroutines** ã the entire assistant section of `Models.kt`, snake_case JSON and all, plus `suspend fun` (Module 3)
- **Jetpack Compose** ã composables, layout, modifiers, `remember`, `StateFlow` view models (Modules 4ã6)
- **A design system** ã the `Hud` tokens and typography on every screen (Module 7)
- **Networking & auth** ã Retrofit, `Network.kt`, the DataStore token, the auth interceptor (Modules 8ã9)
- **Real screens** ã lists, navigation, booking flows, a live map (Modules 10ã12)
- **And today** ã an AI assistant that was, honestly, just all of the above again

That last point is the real graduation gift: the 'hardest' feature in the app introduced *zero* new concepts. That's what knowing a stack feels like ã new features stop being new, and start being combinations.

steps.q: Self-assessment: which pair is matched correctly?
choices: `suspend fun` ã a function that can pause without blocking the thread it's called from
`sealed interface` ã a class that can never be instantiated under any circumstance
`StateFlow` ã a one-time event, like a button click
`remember` ã permanent storage that survives an app restart
steps.explain: `suspend` marks a function that can be paused and resumed without blocking a thread ã that's the whole coroutine story from Module 3. A `sealed interface` *can* be instantiated, just only by the implementations you've declared (Module 2). `StateFlow` holds an ongoing *value* over time, not a one-shot event. `remember` only survives recomposition, not a process death or restart ã that's what `rememberSaveable` or real persistence (DataStore, Module 8) is for.
steps.nudge: Match each term to what it actually guarantees ã not just what it sounds like it might do.

steps.q: Final exam, one question. An owner taps a suggested walker's chip in the assistant chat. What happens, in order?
choices: `onBook(walker)` fires ã the screen that hosts `AssistantScreen` reacts (e.g. by navigating to or showing `CreateBookingScreen` for that walker) ã booking it POSTs to `/bookings`
The chip calls `Network.api.assistantChat` again, this time with the walker's name
The chip directly inserts a row into the backend database
Nothing ã chips are decorative and only the text field can start a booking
